doc_id: PERSONS UNWELL OUTSIDE QUEEN SQUARE BUILDINGS
page_id: PERSONS UNWELL OUTSIDE QUEEN SQUARE BUILDINGS.pdf_p1
text:
## **Clinical Emergencies - Pathway for Persons Unwell Outside Queen Square Buildings** **PPE TO BE WORN BEFORE ANY CONTACT WITH THE INDIVIDUAL** This guideline is aimed at care of persons unwell outside The National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery (NHNN) and The Royal London Hospital for Integrated Medicine (RLHIM) buildings to ensure UCLH responds appropriately to safeguard the wellbeing of such persons and UCLH staff. It is to be used in conjunction with UCLH Cardiac Arrest Policy and is in line with other site-specific guidance at UCLH. It is only applicable to the care of unwell within the **defined boundaries** of Queen persons Square Buildings. There is a separate pathway for clinical emergencies **within** Queen Square sites. ## **GUIDELINES FOR CARE** ## **PERSON UNWELL OUTSIDE QUEEN SQUARE BUILDINGS** **RECEPTION** ( _main NHNN Queen Square or RLHIM entrance)_ alerted that person unwell outside Queen Square building ## **RECEPTION alert EMERGENCY TEAM via 2222** ## **Team members** Outreach Nurse Clinical Site Practitioner Portering staff Medical staff Security staff Security to meet emergency team by main reception NHNN Collect red bag, oxygen and suction from main reception desk and glucometer from nearest clinical area Emergency team, security and porter to attend unwell person together Team will assess clinical state of unwell person (ABCDE) **==> picture [755 x 684] intentionally omitted <==** **----- Start of picture text -----**

Any staff who assist patients within the hospital are covered by the Trust's insurance UCLH will authorise staff and will cover them to provide care within the defined boundaries of the hospital, in the case of emergencies, as long as the health professional acted within their competence Other members of Trust staff may be first to encounter the unwell person – they should ensure that an emergency call has been placed via 2222 Other clinical staff may assist with care in conjunction with the emergency team if they are competent to do so and within the remit of this document If a situation is assessed as posing a risk to staff, then they should not attend the call, and a 999 call should be made to emergency services ## **IF CARDIAC ARREST SITUATION** - Call should go to London Ambulance Service (LAS) Porter returns to main reception and makes cardiac arrest call to LAS via 999 clearly stating location - - - Security staff may also raise 999 call via their radios and control desk - - Emergency Team commence BLS / ALS _(with assistance from UCLH clinical staff in attendance)_ - - If Emergency Team Lead deems it safe to move, unwell person will be lifted to trolley, as per UCLH guidelines, and transferred to place of safety within NHNN - - If unsafe to move, continue acute care in current location until LAS arrives - LAS will take over care when they attend - Termination of resuscitation and end-of-life decisions should not be made until patient is in UCH A+E or under care of paramedic crew - Queen Square Emergency Team do not accompany to UCH A&E - _**If not full cardiac arrest situation but person acutely unwell / peri-arrest follow cardiac arrest call procedure as above**_ THE NATIONAL HOSPITAL FOR NEUROLOGY AND NEUROSURGERY AUGUST 2022

doc_id: Ketamine IV poster NHNN
page_id: Ketamine IV poster NHNN.pdf_p1
text:
## **Low dose ketamine IV infusion** 

Following Complex Spinal Surgery Do usual post-op Vital Signs **To LOCK SCREEN on BBraun pump** Scroll down to OPTIONS, `◄` select 7 DATA LOCK, `◄` select Use Lock-Box on ward Choose level 1 or 2 (controlled drug) Enter Code: **2222** 

Do **NOT** display poster in patient area with **code** written. 

## **IV Ketamine** low dose may continue on wards 

100mg in 50mL (2mg/mL) running at **1.5mL/hr** (3mg/hr) 

Pain Team/Anaesthetist will review daily and advise whether to continue. 

Contact Acute Pain Team / Anaesthetist for: 

Key for lock-box is on CD key bunch 

- Instructions to continue before syringe is finished 

   - may run for up to 72hrs as indicated 

- Any concerns with pain, sedation or any adverse effects. 

## **Ward staff may administer pre-filled syringe.** 

**Will require concurrent simple analgesics plus appropriate opioid analgesics for surgical pain.** 

## **Indication:** 

Following complex spinal surgery 

Following major spinal surgery and patient is opioid tolerant or dependent 

Difficult to manage neuropathic pain post-operatively 

Acute exacerbation of chronic pain who present for major or complex spinal surgery 

**Contraindication:** NEVER give to these patients. 

Patient has ‘brain’ pathology or has had Brain Surgery 

Raised intracranial / intraocular pressure 

Severe liver disease 

Active psychosis 

Porphyria (metabolic disorder) Stroke 

Pregnancy 

Poorly controlled cardio-vascular disease including Uncontrolled Hypertension 

Acute Pain Team (2021)       CNS 07950 856 314,      Anaesthetist oncall bleep 8011.

The National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery Neuroscience Critical Care Unit (NCCU) 

## **PRE-PROCEDURE FASTING** 

These guidelines are to be followed for patients undergoing a procedure that requires fasting or interruption of NG feeding _Pre-operative / pre-procedure – includes operations and procedures in theatre, in NCCU and other departments such as radiology where sedation or general anaesthesia is necessary_ 

- **FASTING PRIOR TO** 

- **FOOD / MILK PLANNED SURGERY / INTERVENTION** 

- ee Includes solid food, soup, milk, milky tea, **NON-INTUBATED PATIENTS** coffee, sweets, NG / PEG feed 

- Be **WATER / CLEAR FLUID** `o` No food or milk for **6 hours** pre-operatively `o` **2 hours** 

- ▪ No chewing gum for Patients should drink clear fluid freely up until 2 hours before procedure `o` No water or clear fluids for **2 hours** pre- 

- ▪ If patient does not like water, other clear operatively 

- fluids are allowed - black tea, diluted juice `o` 

- squash and other non-fizzy clear drinks Essential medications taken as agreed with _(clear = newsprint should be visible_ medical staff _through the glass of liquid)_ 

- ▪ Non-carbonated rehydration drinks are **PATIENTS ON ORAL** 

- good source of carbohydrate and **HYPOGLYCAEMICS / INSULIN** 

- electrolytes and patients without diabetes having elective procedures should be Anaesthesia and surgery can lead to encouraged to drink these complex metabolic and neuroendocrine 

- ▪ Recommended maximum water intake responses and perioperative management of from 6 to 2 hours pre-operatively is diabetic patients requires careful 1000mL for adults 

`o` No food or milk for **6 hours** pre-operatively 

- No chewing gum for **2 hours** pre-operatively 

- `o` No water or clear fluids for **2 hours** pre- 

- operatively 

- Essential medications taken as agreed with medical staff 

Anaesthesia and surgery can lead to complex metabolic and neuroendocrine responses and perioperative management of diabetic patients requires careful assessment, planning and co-ordination _See UCLH guidelines_ 

## **ORAL MEDICATION** 

- Being nil by mouth is not a reason for patients not to take oral medication 

- ▪ Oral medication may be taken during the fasting period with sips of water 

- ▪ Medications should be continued preoperatively following discussion with medical staff 

- **RESTARTING NG FEED** 

- Reconfirm NG tube position prior to restarting NG feed in all cases 

- fn ———————————————EE—EE—EEEEEeEeeeeeee **Restart at pre-procedure rate** ▪ **Immediately on return** after nonabdominal surgery, scans, interventional neuroradiology with a protected airway 

- ▪ **4 hours** after airway procedures such as tracheostomy or extubation if stable 

**STOPPING ENTERAL NUTRITION PRIOR TO PLANNED SURGERY / INTERVENTION INTUBATED PATIENTS** and **PATIENTS** with **TRACHEOSTOMY BEFORE NON-AIRWAY SURGERY** (Neurosurgery) / **INTERVENTIONAL NEURORADIOLOGY** 

- **NO FASTING** – enteral feed continued until preparing patient for procedure 

- **NGT ASPIRATED** , gastric contents discarded and tube capped prior to leaving Neurocritical Care Unit (NCCU) 

## **BEFORE AIRWAY PROCEDURES** 

- _Percutaneous or Surgical Tracheostomy / Airway Change / Extubation / Bronchoscopy_ `o` Time for procedure should be confirmed 

- Enteral feed stopped **4 hours** prior to procedure 

- If patient to be on emergency list, fast from **0400** as timing for procedure may be subject to change 

- **NGT ASPIRATED** , gastric contents discarded and tube capped prior to leaving NCCU 

## **BEFORE CT SCAN / MRI** 

- **NO FASTING** – enteral feed continued until preparing patient to leave NCCU – _exception PET scan which requires 6 hours fasting to ensure empty stomach_ 

- **NGT ASPIRATED** , gastric contents discarded and tube capped prior to leaving NCCU 

## **BEFORE TRANSOESOPHAGEAL ECHO** (TOE) 

- Time for procedure should be confirmed 

- Enteral feed stopped **4 hours** prior to procedure 

- **NGT ASPIRATED** and gastric contents discarded immediately prior to procedure 

THE NATIONAL HOSPITAL FOR NEUROLOGY AND NEUROSURGERY AUGUST 2023

doc_id: THIOPENTAL IN ACUTE BRAIN INJURY
page_id: THIOPENTAL IN ACUTE BRAIN INJURY.pdf_p1
text:
The National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery Neuroscience Critical Care Unit (NCCU) - **THIOPENTAL** for **REFRACTORY RAISED ICP** in **ACUTE BRAIN INJURY** INDICATIONS FOR USE Thiopental is a rapidly acting barbiturate used mainly for induction of anaesthesia It can be used in patients with acute brain injury to control intractable intracranial hypertension (by decreasing the metabolic demands of the brain) when other conventional medical and simple surgical methods to lower ICP are ineffective. It may be used as a last resort before carrying out decompressive craniectomy. Thiopental is only commenced when all other conventional medical measures to control ICP have failed. ## STABILITY AND COMPATIBILITY - Reconstituted solutions of Thiopental should be discarded after 24 hours • Thiopental should not be given with any other drugs • Before commencing ensure patient adequately hydrated as hypovolaemia may precipitate hypotension ## COMPLICATIONS OF PROLONGED THERAPY |**HIGH DOSE THIOPENTAL in REFRACTORY INTRACRANIAL HYPERTENSION**
_ONLY USED WHEN ALL OTHER MEASURES HAVE FAILED TO CONTROL ICP_| |---| |**STEP 1**
• Thiopental is administered as a25mg/ml(2.5%) solution
• Reconstitute each 500mg vial with 20ml of water for injection
• For infusions - reconstitute 3 x 500mg vials of Thiopental with 60ml of water for injection
• Preferably given via a central line
**LOADING DOSE**| |**STEP 2**
• Give**250mg**IV bolus over**5 minutes**
• Repeat until a reduction in ICP is observed – up to a**maximum of 3 bolus doses**
• When a reduction is seen commence high dose infusion_(see below)_
• _Bolus acts within one arm-brain circulation time (I.e. 1 minute), effect lasts for 5-15 minutes and is_
_cumulative after repeated administration_
**_If no reduction in ICP after 3 bolus doses infusion should NOT be commenced_**| Prolonged clinical effect especially in patients with impaired renal / hepatic function Hypotension – implications for CPP therefore reduce other sedative drugs ## **L THERAPY** ## Loss of pupillary reactivity – pupils can become fixed For brain-stem testing, levels must be obtained _(thiopental level must_ _**zero** be <5mg/l – but a level of might be required if patient has acute kidney injury)_ ## **INFUSION** - **STEP 3** • Commence infusion at **3-5mg/kg/h** _(Higher doses must be discussed with NCC consultant prior to increasing infusion)_ - • Infusion rate should be titrated to achieve **ICP <22mmHg** - • If infusion is effective other sedation should be **reduced** to maintain CPP and limit noradrenaline requirements - • Infusion should continue for **maximum 48 hours** without further discussion with NCCU Consultant THE NATIONAL HOSPITAL FOR NEUROLOGY AND NEUROSURGERY JULY 2023

text:
## The National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery Neuroscience Critical Care Unit (NCCU) ## **HYPEROSMOLAR THERAPY** ## **HYPERTONIC SALINE 2.7%** and **MANNITOL 15%** Both 2.7% hypertonic saline (HTS) and mannitol 15% can effectively reduce intracranial pressure, but HTS has a more sustained effect on intracranial pressure and can effectively increase cerebral perfusion pressure and is therefore the 1[st] choice hyperosmolar therapy. as Hyperosmolar therapy should be given as directed by neurosurgeon, NCCU consultant or anaesthetist via **large bore cannula** (14G or 16G) stat dose over **30min** followed by reassessment of pupils before further dosing. Hyperosmolar therapy is most commonly used in the event of a ‘blown pupil’. A “ **blown pupil** ” is a medical emergency indicating trans-tentorial herniation and brainstem compression. This can be caused by a variety of surgical reversible pathologies such as haematoma, hydrocephalus, large volume infarct or oedema surrounding tumour or infection. Rapid access to surgical evacuation of extra-axial haematomas, significantly improves outcomes and is the most important factor in improving prognosis. Progression to definitive surgical intervention must occur within ‘the . golden hour’ to prevent secondary brain injury and irreversible brainstem ischaemia In event of a ‘blown pupil’ follow BLOWN PUPIL PATHWAY ## **1[st] LINE: 2.7% HYPERTONIC SALINE** (3-5ml/kg) _**DO NOT** give to patients with known hypernatraemia (Serum Sodium >155mmol/L)_ ## **Effects and Evidence for use** - Hypertonic saline (HTS) restores intravascular volume and may increase BP as well as lowering ICP - HTS has a rapid onset, ≈ 5 minutes, with effects lasting up to 12 hours without rebound ICP increase (may be some rebound increase in ICP but this side effect is limited) - HTS provides a more rapid and sustained ICP reduction compared with mannitol - Osmotic effect - mechanism of action predominantly through marked osmotic shift of fluid from intracellular to interstitial and intravascular space - Hypertonic saline causes hypernatraemia – therefore serum sodium should be measured within 6 hours of HTS administration if bolus doses given and measured regularly if infusion commenced **Initial stat dose of 210ml over 30min STEP 1** - _In a typical 70kg patient 210ml = ~3ml/kg_ Reassessment of pupils prior to further dosing **STEP 2** on medical instruction • Re-administration of hypertonic saline should not occur until serum sodium concentration is <155mmol/L • Acute hyperosmolar state - most feared complication is central pontine myelinolysis, also known as ODS (osmotic demyelination syndrome) in hyponatremic states when sodium levels have been corrected rapidly • Extravasation injuries may cause marked local tissue injury, therefore check infusion site regularly or give via central line if available ## **15% MANNITOL (** 0.5-1g/kg) _Sugar alcohol hypertonic solution_ ## **Effects and Evidence for use** - Reduces blood viscosity, increases CBF and increases cerebral oxygen delivery - - Reduces ICP within a few minutes _(most marked when CPP low (<70mmHg)_ - - can cause - Osmotic diuretic (e _ffect delayed for 15-30min, persists for 90min to 6h or more) -_ hypotension secondary to osmotic diuresis, which could potentially be deleterious in patients who are hypotensive or hypovolaemic - Large doses of Mannitol _1.8g/kg-2.1g/kg_ effective in comatose patients with operative haematoma _(Level I evidence = High degree of clinical certainty)_ - 1.2-1.4g/kg in comatose trauma patients with operative ASDH or operative intraparenchymal temporal lobe haemorrhage _(Level I recommendation)_ - 0.25-1g/kg to control elevated ICP in severe TBI _(Level II recommendation)_ infused over at least 15 minutes _(May be most effective when CPP <70mmHg (Level II recommendation)_ - • Bolus dose at rate not exceeding 0.1g/kg/min to avoid risk of sudden hypotension ( _Level II recommendation)_ - • Intermittent boluses may be more effective than continuous infusion ( _Level III recommendation)_ - **Initial stat dose of 230ml over 30min** - **STEP 1** - _In a typical 70kg patient 230ml = ~ 0.5g/kg_ - Reassessment of pupils prior to further dosing - **STEP 2** on medical instruction - **Mannitol intoxication and the ‘Rebound Effect’** ▪ When infusion rate exceeds rate of hourly excretion in urine, mannitol accumulates in extracellular space - ▪ Breakdown of blood-brain barrier and extravasation into interstitium of brain thought to result in reverse osmotic gradient that can cause cerebral oedema - ▪ Risk may be greater with continuous rather than bolus infusions - ▪ Monitor electrolytes, fluid balance and serum and urine osmolarity _**Calculations for bolus dose based on lowest recommended doses**_ |**MANNITOL 15%**

image_path: /content/page_images/METARAMINOL FOR RAPID CONTROL OF ACUTE HYPOTENSION AUGUST 2023.pdf_p1.jpg
text:
ae Pelee aa - a The National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery . ie on NERO ; University College -=f‘3 ey4Fii Neuroscience Critical Care Unit (NCCU) Lonaon Hospita > 3 iba[|] 4 ei-a7: ieIHS F oOundatTiondq Trust i ai a fy r at **METARAMINOL** "Fe. t _— FS Wien neg cae ant INDICATIONS FOR USE Metaraminol is the vasoconstrictor of choice for the short-term **METARAMINOL for SHORT-TERM MANAGEMENT of** management of acute hypotension and can be administered by peripheral **ACUTE HYPOTENSION** intravenous catheter. Onset of action: 1–2 minutes, Duration of action: 20– 60 minutes, Half-life: minutes. 

Metaraminol is an intravenous vasopressor that increases both systolic and diastolic BP. The pressor effect of a single dose of metaraminol lasts from about 20 minutes up to one hour. Its onset is around one or two minutes after direct intravenous injection. The vasopressor effects taper off when therapy is stopped. 

It can be given IV peripherally to treat hypotension or support BP and can be given as a BOLUS. 

METARAMINOL DOES **NOT** NEED TO BE ‘DOUBLE PUMPED’ 

`o` Short period of disconnection will not cause a drop in BP 

`o` An existing syringe can be disconnected and a new one replaced within the space of 3-4min without a significant drop in BP 

FACTORS THAT WILL DETERMINE SWITCHING TO NORADRENALINE 

- If the need for vasopressors is going to exceed 48 hours 

- If the metaraminol dose requirement is high and increasing, needing frequent syringe changes and high volumes 

- If a central line is inserted for other reasons, metaraminol should be switched to noradrenaline 

## COMPATABILITY and STABILITY 

Compatible with 0.9% Sodium chloride / 5% Dextrose / Hartmann’s Solution / 4% dextrose / 0.18% saline 

- Metaraminol should be given through a dedicated peripheral line. 

- `o` The standard dilution is to produce a solution containing 0.5mg/ml (2 ampoules of 10mg/mL diluted in 0.9% sodium chloride to make a total of 40ml) 

- The reconstituted solution should be used immediately but is stable in the syringe for up to 24 hours - _discard unused solutions after 24 hours_ 

- CAUTIONS and CONTRAINDICATIONS 

- Use with caution in patients taking digoxin as the combination may provoke arrhythmia 

- Monoamine oxidase inhibitors and tricyclic antidepressants may potentiate the action of metaraminol  infusion should be titrated to effect with care 

**PRESENTATION** 1 Ampoule of metaraminol tartrate contains **10mg in 1ml** solution for injection **STEP 1 ADMINISTRATION** Dilute **20mg** of metaraminol (2 ampoules of 10mg/mL) with 0.9% sodium chloride to produce a total solution of _40ml (20mg/40mL =_ _**0.5mg/m** L)_ Deliver by syringe pump peripherally ( _via_ _**dedicated peripheral intravenous l**_ **ine) STEP 2 BOLUS DOSE If required,** an initial **bolus dose of 1ml (0.5mg)** may be given by medical staff | **STEP 3 INFUSION** Continuous infusion of 20mg/40mL = **0.5mg/m** L titrated to BP target Start the infusion at rate of **2ml/h = 1mg/h Increase infusion rate by 2ml/h every 15 minutes** until BP target reached - _If requiring >20ml/h inform medical staff_ **STEP 4 WEANING** When target BP is consistently achieved with only **1-2ml/h** of metaraminol, the infusion can be safely discontinued The primed line should be left connected on the syringe driver in case the infusion needs to be restarted within  the following hour If BP target still maintained after **1 HOUR** the metaraminol infusion can be disconnected and discarded The cannula should then be flushed to remove traces of residual drug 

## SIDE EFFECTS 

- Sinus or ventricular tachycardia 

- `o` Bolus dose can cause marked hypertension and bradycardia 

- `o` Abscess formation and tissue necrosis at site of injection 

- `o` Malaria relapse in those patients with previous exposure 

- `o` Conscious patients may complain of headache 

THE NATIONAL HOSPITAL FOR NEUROLOGY AND NEUROSURGERY JULY 2023

text:
## The National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery Neuroscience Critical Care Unit (NCCU) **ICP / CPP DIRECTED THERAPY** in **ACUTE BRAIN INJURY (ABI)** This protocol outlines safe targets based on agreed guidelines, which may be adapted in individual clinical circumstances with the judgement / knowledge of the responsible senior clinician ## **INTRACRANIAL HYPERTENSION MANAGEMENT** Treatment of elevated intracranial pressure (ICP) and cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP) is common in patients with neurological injury. ICP / CPP monitoring has been most extensively studied in severe traumatic brain injury (TBI) and is widely used as the standard of care. ## ► **BASIC NEUROPROTECTIVE MEASURES** - Neuroprotective measures involve early, aggressive control of factors implicated in secondary brain injury - A. Artificial Ventilation / Sedation / Analgesia - _propofol & fentanyl infusions_ - B. Aim for **PaO 11kPa – 13kPa PaCO 4.5 – 5.0kPa 2** , **2** It can play a similar role in other patients with Acute Brain Injury (ABI) such as subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH), intracerebral haemorrhage (ICH) and acute ischaemic stroke (AIS) to help direct medical and surgical therapy. - _Mandatory correction of hypoxia (defined as SaO2 <8kPa, cyanosis or apnoea)_ - ► _Avoid PaCO <4kPa 2_ - _Avoid sustained hyperoxaemia (defined as PaO_ _**2** >16kPa)_ ## C. **EUVOLAEMIA** IV crystalloids aiming for _(or slight hypervolaemia)_ - _Mandatory correction of hypotension (defined as SBP <90mmHg)_ The threshold that defines intracranial hypertension is uncertain, but it is generally agreed that an **ICP >22mmHg** should be treated and ICP / CPP monitoring are strongly recommended in all patients at risk. - _BP management according to disease specific targets but avoiding hypo / hypertension in acute phase_ - _Ensure adequate fluid resuscitation (guided by FlowTrac monitoring) before starting vasopressors_ - D. GCS, pupil, limb assessment _(clinical assessment depending on level of sedation)_ - _ICP monitoring recommended in all patients at risk of intracranial hypertension_ The threshold for ICP in different intracranial pathologies remains controversial. There is no clear evidence for ICPguided therapy, or whether ICP should be monitored, when the patient has undergone **decompressive craniectomy** (DC) with some studies suggesting that the ICP treatment threshold should be lower in patients with an ‘open skull’. - E. Blood glucose **7 – 12mmol/L** - _Treat hypoglycaemia & hyperglycaemia (see Blood Glucose Protocol)_ ## Head up **30 – 45[o]** - _Head up 30 - 45[o ] optimal for most ABI patients (if hypotension corrected & spinal injury excluded)_ - _Head & neck in neutral alignment, avoiding hyperflexion & hyperextension_ - ► _Not >45[o ] head up_ ## **SETTING TARGETS** **NORMOTHERMIA 37.0 C** ± **0.5 C** Temperature – (defined as ) Monitoring several intracranial variables at the same time (multimodality monitoring – MMM) can complicate decision making in setting targets. Patients with ABI will have prescribed targets based on presenting pathology and their subsequent highest risk – _haemorrhage / ischaemia / oedema etc._ Some patients have conflicting targets and decisions must reflect the key targets to adhere to _**(hierarchy of targets)**_ when achieving all set targets proves difficult. Decisions should always be based on greatest risk. - _Treat pyrexia (temperature >37.5C) with antipyretic agent (paracetamol)_ - _Targeted Temperature Management i(TTM) if not resolved 2h after administration (see TTM Protocol)_ ## **THERAPEUTIC GOALS ONCE ICP MONITORING COMMENCED** ► Target ICP **<22mmHg** ( _Treat ICP >22mmHg) The Brain Trauma Foundation Guidelines ICP >22mmHg for prolonged period associated with poorer outcome, therefore BTF guidelines favour ICP <20mmHg & recommend_ _**treating** ICP >22mmHg_ **==> picture [610 x 28] intentionally omitted <==** Consider _**phase of injury**_ when setting targets: |**_phase of injury_**when setting targets:
- **Acute phase (**∼ **24-96h)**set targets_(also consider after any_
_subsequent intracranial event)_
- **Subacute phase (**∼ **5-14 days)**consider relaxing targets
- **Post-acute phase (after**∼**14 days)**consider need for targets
There are always risks in**overachieving** as well as in not
meeting a set target.
**Class**
of**Recommendation**
►**Class 1 (Level I) : STRONG**
_Recommended (Benefit >>> Risk)_
►**Class 2a (Level IIA): MODERATE**
_Reasonable (Benefit >> Risk)_
►**Class 2b (Level IIB / III): WEAK**
_May be reasonable_(Benefit ≥ Risk)
►**Class 3: STRONG**
_Potential harm (Risk > Benefit)_

c| |---|---|---| **SEDATION** and **ANALGESIA** Use **SEDLINE** monitor to guide sedative doses Once burst suppression (state of deep inactivity in the brain / cortical silence) reached, there is no clinical indication to increase sedation further Infusions above upper limit stated below must be approved by the NCC consultant 0.5mg/kg test bolus **PROPOFOL** 2% 20-75mcg/kg/min infusion _20mg/mL_ ∼ _For 70k atient 4-15mL/h g p_ 2mcg/kg test dose **FENTANYL** 2-5mcg/kg/h infusion _50mcg/mL_ ∼ _For 70k atient_ 2-7mL/h _g p_ 2mg test dose **MIDAZOLAM** 30-100mcg/kg/h infusion _1mg/mL_ ∼ _For 70k atient 2-7mL/h g p_ Due to potential toxic side effects, duration and dose of any drug must be carefully monitored, Commence at lowest dose and titrate to achieve ICP target ## **PROPOFOL** **Propofol Infusion Syndrome (PRIS)** Consider in all patients on propofol, especially doses >75mcg/kg/min or any dose when duration exceeds 48h Screen daily for CK, unexplained acidosis or ECG changes **NEUROMUSCULAR BLOCKING AGENTS** These have no direct effect on ICP but prevent rises produced by coughing on ETT – _ensure sedation levels adequate_ ## _**Atracurium**_ Give bolus dose / observe effect on ICP If effective commence infusion (0.5mg/kg/h) If history of asthma use vecuronium Propofol and NMBAs not compatible (use separate IV lines) Seizures difficult to detect if paralysed ( _may be signaled by bilateral pupillary dilatation, small increase in ABP_ ## **THIOPENTONE** Only used when all other methods to reduce ICP have failed _(see Thiopentone protocol)_ THE NATIONAL HOSPITAL FOR NEUROLOGY AND NEUROSURGERY JULY 2024

page_id: ORGAN DONATION (DCD) ES + DM +DL revision Mar2024-3.pdf_p1
text:
The National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery Neuroscience Critical Care Unit (NCCU) 

- a7arymii ® £ **Death** by **Circulatory Death (DCD) - APPROACHING FAMILIES** of **POTENTIAL DCD** 

- atpoe 

- ts **DONORS FOLLOWING WITHDRAWAL** of **TREATMENT DECISION** 

- we ra+t 7 _(Collaborative Approach)_ Once decision made by **Neurocritical Care (NCC) MDT** to withdraw life-sustaining treatment in a patient with life-threatening or life-limiting condition which will, or is expected to, result in circulatory death, refer to the Embedded Specialist Nurse – Organ Donation (SNOD) or on call SNOD – Pager 

- **NCC Nurse, NCC Consultant +/- SNOD discuss MDT decision / plan to withdraw treatment with family DONATION DISCUSSION IS NOT INITIATED AT THIS TIME** 

- ▪ When family agree with the MDT opinion that continuing treatment is not in patient’s best interests, NCC Consultant, NCC Nurse and SNOD discuss donation with family 

- ▪ SNOD gives family information on patient’s wishes, where known Organ donation should 

- ▪ Family supported throughout decision-making process 

- ▪ Family given relevant information and any questions addressed at this time 

- ▪ Handprints / hair locks can be offered to family at this time _(see SWAN box)_ and death certificate completed if **NOT** coroner’s case 

Once decision made by **Neurocritical Care (NCC) MDT** to withdraw life-sustaining treatment in a patient with life-threatening or life-limiting condition which **03000 20 30 40** will, or is expected to, result in circulatory death, refer to the Embedded Specialist Nurse – Organ Donation (SNOD) or on call SNOD – Pager 

## **REFERRAL** to **SNOD** 

The medical decision made by NCC Consultant and referring teams that continuing life sustaining treatment no longer in patient’s best interests, but treatment will not yet have been withdrawn 

Organ donation should **NOT** be discussed in same conversation as withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment unless those close to patient initiate these discussions Vital that ventilation continues, and that patient is **NOT** extubated at point the referral is made Ultimate responsibility for referral of patient to SNOD lies with NCC Consultant - _however, any staff member involved in the care of the atient can make the referral p_ **WITHDRAWAL** of **TREATMENT** 

- If family consent to donation the SNOD works with the MDT to optimise the patient for donation as per ‘Management of Patient For Organ Donation’ 

- ► It is acceptable to maintain the existing level of support, and consider introduction / escalation of treatment if needed _(Donation Actions Framework 2022)_ 

- ► NCC medical staff contact Duty Medical Examiner - _all NCC deaths discussed with UCLH Medical Examiner – via email: uclh.medicalexaminer@nhs.net / or Tel: 020 344(75129) and 020 344(75130) Monday – Friday   During extended hours please call 020 3086 7056, 9am-12noon_ 

- ► The SNOD: ‒ informs family of process of organ donation 

- ‒ completes Consent and Patient Assessment with family 

- ‒ contacts Coroner and GP _(where able)_ 

- ‒ undertakes physical examination and assessment of patient 

- ‒ takes blood samples for virology and tissue typing 

- ► The patient is registered with Hub Operations, where matching process commenced and organs offered - SNOD will liaise with theatres, anaesthetics and organise retrieval teams to attend hospital 

- ► Once retrieval teams are ready, withdrawal takes place either in NCC _(1[st] floor unit if possible)_ or in the anaesthetic room and this decision should be taken by the clinical team on duty – _NCC nurse remains responsible for patient (wherever withdrawal takes place) until patient certified dead_ 

- ► If cardiac / lung retrieval planned, extubation **must** take place in the anaesthetic room ► Family can be present at this time, either in NCC or in the anaesthetic room ► Following loss of cardiac output, patient monitored for further **5 minutes** ► Certification of death in accordance with usual cardiorespiratory criteria either by the Anaesthetic SpR attending in theatre or by a member of NCC medical team (ST3 or above) who knows the patient 

- ► If, at this time, family do not wish to leave, retrieval teams stand down and donation process stopped ► If retrieval process stopped, patient will return to NCC for ‘After Death Care’ ► If donation proceeds where lung retrieval anticipated, patient will be reintubated post confirmation of circulatory death, prior to retrieval process – _but lung insufflation will only be performed 10 minutes after loss of output_ 

- ► If donation proceeds, once completed, SNOD performs ‘After Death Care’ in theatre, with theatre staff and organises transfer to mortuary 

- ► If patient does not die within stipulated time, they return to NCC for continuing ‘end of life’ care 

If family decide donation is not right option for them, they are thanked for considering donation and treatment is discontinued _**Refer to palliative extubation checklist**_ Family are supported by NCC nurse +/- SNOD If patient dies within short time, ‘After Death Care’ performed If death not imminent, arrangements may be made to transfer patient to a side room on a ward 

The continuation of good end of life care, including measures to maintain the comfort and dignity of the patient should **never** be compromised for the purposes of facilitating organ donation 

Once consent has been obtained it is acceptable to maintain the existing level of support, and introduction of additional treatment may also be considered Neither the SNOD nor any member of the surgical retrieval team will be involved with the withdrawal process 

## **CERTIFYING DEATH** 

Following onset of **monitored loss of output** , patient should continue to be monitored for 5 minutes. If, during this time, there is any return of **cardiac output** a further 5 minutes of observation should recommence. 

At the conclusion of 5 minutes of monitored asystole death can be certified in accordance with usual cardiorespiratory criteria by an appropriately trained doctor involved in the patient’s care. 

In circumstances where lung donation is anticipated, the anaesthetist will be required to reintubate the patient in theatre after confirmation of death and prior to the retrieval process. 

THE NATIONAL HOSPITAL FOR NEUROLOGY AND NEUROSURGERY APRIL 2024

page_id: BRAINSTEM DEATH 2 PRECONDITIONS AND EXCLUSIONS JANUARY 2025.pdf_p1
text:
## The National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery Neuroscience Critical Care Unit (NCCU) 

**2. DIAGNOSING DEATH** by **NEUROLOGICAL CRITERIA (DNC) – Preconditions / Exclusions** Academy of Medical Royal Colleges (2025) Code of Practice in UK 

## **PRECONDITIONS** 

## **Specific preconditions for the application of neurological criteria must be fulfilled before doctors commence clinical testing** 

- ─ Aetiology severe enough to cause permanent cessation of brainstem function _Should be no doubt that condition due to devastating brain injury of known aetiology / mechanism_ 

- ─ Assessment period sufficient to exclude potential for recovery 

   - _In primary intracranial pathology at least 6h following loss of last observed brainstem reflex or spontaneous breath In acute hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy or post cardiorespiratory arrest at least 24h  following loss of last observed brainstem reflex or spontaneous breath_ 

- ─ Exclusion of potentially reversible factors contributing to coma or apnoea _Factors which risk confounding diagnosis of death by neurological criteria must be excluded (see Exclusions below)_ 

- ─ Caution diagnosing DNC in uncommon circumstances 

   - _If aetiology primarily posterior fossa / brainstem then MRI recommended prior to DNC_ 

   - _Initially isolated posterior fossa lesion may develop supratentorial pathology and standard approaches to neurological criteria would then apply_ 

   - _Agreement in MDT that no further appropriate therapeutic options of benefit and enough time has elapsed to exclude possibility of neurological recovery_ 

## **EXCLUSIONS** 

## Hypothermia 

- ° 

- ─ Core temperature  ≥ 36 C at time of clinical testing  - may require active warming 

   - _Hypothermia may delay return of brainstem function - neurological criteria should not be applied until at least 24h following correction of hypothermia (core temperature of ≥36_ ° _C)_ ° 

   - • _Following correction of hypothermia any transient and temporary reductions in temperature to below 36 C do not mandate further 24h observation at normothermia_ 

## Depressant drugs 

- ─ Patients with devastating brain injury are considered as having a vulnerable brain which might be more sensitive to lower levels of depressant drugs 

- ─ Exclude possibility of ongoing drug effect being cause of, or materially contributing to, coma or apnoea 

## Profound neuromuscular weakness 

- ─ Neuromuscular weakness resembling absence of brainstem reflexes may occur as consequence of neuromuscular or metabolic disorders, including critical illness neuromyopathies, or be the result of the effect of some drugs: 

   - _Neuromuscular blocking agents and other drugs must be excluded_ 

   - _Peripheral nerve stimulator or presence of deep-tendon reflexes, electromyography to confirm that adequate neuromuscular function present_ 

   - _Where pre-existing or acquired neuromuscular disorder known or possible, consideration given to impact of drug history and muscle function before applying neurological criteria_ 

## Cervical spinal cord pathology 

- ─ If _**high**_ cervical cord pathology +/- associated cervical spine injury, contributing to apnoea, further investigation (CT or MRI) needed 

   - _Where imaging demonstrates cervical spinal cord pathology, apnoea test alone cannot reliably confirm loss of capacity to breathe and must be supplemented with an ancillary investigation_ 

- ─ In cervical spinal cord pathology, apnoea test can still be performed unless injury of such an extent that spontaneous breathing considered impossible 

## Circulatory and respiratory disturbances 

- ─ Normal (or patient baseline) physiological parameters prior to and during testing 

- ─ Doctors undertaking clinical testing satisfied that any disturbance is not materially contributing to the observed coma or apnoea 

**EXCLUSIONS** 

|**METABOLIC / ENDOCRINE**|**TARGET**|
|---|---|
|HYPOTENSION|MAP >60mmHg|
|HYPOTHERMIA|Minimum Core Temperature 36oC|
|HYPO / HYPERNATRAEMIA|Na+125-160mmol/L (inclusive)|
|HYPOKALAEMIA|K+ >2mmol/L|
|HYPO / HYPERPHOSPHALAEMIA|PO40.5-3.0mmol/L (inclusive)|
|HYPO / HYPERMAGNESAEMIA|Mg2+0.5-3.0mmol/L (inclusive)|
|HYPO / HYPERGLYCAEMIA|BG 3--20mmol/L (inclusive)|
|ACIDAEMIA / ALKALAEMIA|pH 7.35-7.45 / H+45-35mmol/L|



## _Sodium_ 

- ─ Effects of hyponatraemia depend on rate of development, but rare for patients to become unresponsive if sodium >115mmol/L 

      - _If severe hyponatraemia corrected too rapidly patient may develop osmotic demyelination syndrome, which can cause potentially reversible coma_ 

   - 

   - _Sodium levels above 160 mmol/L can be associated with unresponsiveness_ 

## _Potassium_ 

- ─ Very low levels of potassium may cause myopathy and levels <1.0 mmol/L have been reported to cause flaccid quadriplegia 

## _Phosphate and magnesium_ 

- ─ Profound elevation or lowering of phosphate or magnesium may be associated with severe neuromuscular weakness that may culminate in flaccid quadriplegia 

## _Glucose_ 

- ─ As blood glucose concentrations can change rapidly in the critically Ill, blood sugar measurement should be made prior to clinical testing 

   - _Hyperglycaemia in diabetic ketoacidosis or hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state may cause a state of unresponsiveness and cessation of brainstem function, but extremely unlikely with blood glucose levels ≤20.0mmol/L_ 

      - _Severe hypoglycaemia is associated with coma or stupor and testing of brainstem reflexes should not be undertaken if glucose level below 3.0mmol/L_ 

   - 

## _Other electrolyte and metabolic disturbances_ 

## Disturbances may rarely contribute to coma or apnoea 

- _Disturbances such as elevated urea or ammonia, metabolic disorders_ 

   - _Consider correction, expert metabolic advice, or ancillary investigation_ 

- 

## _Endocrine disturbances_ 

Should be satisfied no clinical reason to suspect an endocrine disturbance is materially contributing to coma or apnoea - _If doubt, endocrine assays undertaken_ 

   - _Myxoedema may cause a deep unresponsive coma_ 

- 

_Patients in thyroid storm may present in acute coma or with acute thyrotoxic myopathy_ 

- 

   - _Addisonian crisis may be associated with severe neuromuscular weakness causing an acute ascending paralysis or encephalopathy proceeding to coma_ 

- 

## **‘RED FLAG’ GROUPS** 

Diagnostic caution advised in the following ‘ **RED FLAG’** groups _Consider need to delay testing and / or perform ancillary investigations_ 

1. Testing <6 hours of loss of last brainstem reflex 2. Testing <24 hours of loss of last brainstem reflex, where aetiology primarily anoxic damage 

3. If prior treatment has included hypothermia – 24 hours observation following re-warming to normothermia (36[o] C) recommended 

   - Patients with any neuromuscular disorder 

4. 

5. When steroids given in space occupying lesions, such as abscesses 

   - Prolonged fentanyl infusions 

6. 

7. Aetiology primarily located to brainstem or posterior fossa 

8. Therapeutic decompressive craniectomy – _with resultant change in intracranial dynamics_ 

   - Associated upper cervical spinal cord injury 

9. 

## **The Role of Ancillary Investigations** 

In certain situations, it may not be possible to satisfy all the preconditions or perform or complete all the clinical tests necessary to diagnose death using neurological criteria. 

In such circumstances, ancillary investigation, either before or after clinical testing, is necessary to support a diagnosis of death using neurological criteria.  No single ancillary investigation has worldwide consensus, though there has been a shift toward ancillary investigations that confirm the absence of cerebral blood flow or cerebral perfusion. The 2025 Code provides the principles for the use of ancillary investigations rather than to advise on the specifics of any individual ancillary investigation or protocol. Electroencephalography (EEG) is no longer recommended as an ancillary investigation for neurological criteria in the UK for adults or children. 

UK consensus guideline for the use of cerebral CT angiography as an ancillary investigation to support a clinical diagnosis of death using neurological criteria in adults has been published. 

The 2025 Code recommends that ancillary investigation is required in the following circumstances: ─ When a comprehensive neurological examination, including the apnoea test, is not possible (e.g. high cervical cord pathology, inability to examine both eyes or both ears) ─ When continuing effects of confounding factors which affect the preconditions cannot be excluded (e.g. residual sedation, metabolic or pharmacological derangement, decompressive craniectomy) 

Ancillary investigation should also be considered when there is uncertainty regarding the interpretation of . presumed non-brain mediated movements 

THE NATIONAL HOSPITAL FOR NEUROLOGY AND NEUROSURGERY JANUARY 2025

page_id: KETAMINE INFUSION (002).pdf_p1
text:
## The National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery Neuroscience Critical Care Unit (NCCU) 

- **KETAMINE – POST-OPERATIVE LOW DOSE INTRAVENOUS ADMINISTRATION** 

May only be prescribed on advice of Anaesthetist or Acute Pain Team _(See NHNN Acute Pain Management Guidelines, Appendix 11)_ 

Low dose ketamine infusion may be administered in Recovery, Neurocritical Care and wards at NHNN 

## **INDICATIONS FOR USE** 

- Expected post-operative pain to be severe (following major complex spinal surgery) 

- Expected postoperative pain to be moderate or major but patient is opioid tolerant or dependent (on high doses >100MME pre-operatively) 

- Patients with acute exacerbation of chronic pain who present for moderate or major surgery 

- Patients at high risk of opioid-related side effects (respiratory depression, obstructive sleep apnoea or severe postoperative nausea and vomiting), requiring opioid sparing techniques 

- Patients at high risk of developing chronic post-operative pain (uncontrolled pre or postoperative pain, nerve damage, recent chemo or radiotherapy) 

- **CONTRAINDICATIONS** 

- Do **NOT** give to patients in the following categories `o` Following intracranial surgery 

   - Raised intracranial and intraocular pressure 

   - `o` Poorly controlled cardiovascular disease including uncontrolled hypertension 

   - Severe liver disease (cirrhosis) 

   - Active psychosis 

   - `o` History of ketamine abuse 

   - Pregnancy 

## **ADVERSE EFFECTS** 

Adverse effects following anaesthetic doses of ketamine are listed in the BNF - _Anaesthetic doses are up to 60 x the amount given as low-dose intravenous infusion – therefore at 3mg/h likelihood of these side effects exceptionally rare_ 

- **a) Common or very common:** Diplopia; hallucinations; hypertension; nausea; nightmares; nystagmus; rash; tachycardia; transient psychotic effects; vomiting 

- **b) Uncommon:** Arrhythmias; bradycardia; hypotension; laryngospasm; respiratory depression 

- **c) Rare:** Apnoea; cystitis; haemorrhagic cystitis; hypersalivation; insomnia 

- **d) Frequency not known:** Raised intra-ocular pressure 

For any episode of adverse effects discontinue the ketamine infusion and contact on-call Anaesthetist (bleep 8131 )to review patient  - Datix incident report should be completed 

**ANAESTHETIST** or or **RECOVERY, NCC, ODP/ODA ACUTE PAIN CNS) RESPONSIBLE FOR PREPARING KETAMINE INFUSION** Prepare 100mg IV ketamine made up to 50ml with 0.9% sodium **STEP 1** chloride (2mg/ml) to run at **1.5mL/h** (3mg/h) **LOADING DOSE** Immediately prior to commencing infusion give a **3mg IV bolus** of **STEP 2** ketamine >» 3 **INFUSION STEP 3** Commence IV infusion at **3mg/h (1.5ml/h)** Continue ketamine infusion until syringe runs out or any medical reasons to stop infusion / or as prescribed up to 72 hours maximum `o` _Use giving-set with anti-syphon valve to administer ketamine infusion to prevent drug from free-flowing into patient_ `o` _To prevent back-tracking, anti-reflux valve, sometimes called non-return valve or back-check valve, should be used on extension lines where gravity set may be connected_ `o` _Patients with ketamine infusion may go to a neurosurgical ward as part of their multi-modal analgesia regime_ `o` _Syringe pump must be contained within a lockbox to prevent unauthorised access to the infusion_ `o` _May be administered concurrently with PCA in a PCA approved clinical area_ `o` _May be administered concurrently with morphine infusion in areas that administer morphine infusions_ **DISCONTINUATION OF INFUSION** `o` Discontinue after 24 hours or sooner if pain completely resolves or patient does not tolerate treatment `o` If Anaesthetist or Acute Pain Team recommends continuation of low dose ketamine then a new syringe must be prepared every 24 hours `o` It is not expected that the Ketamine infusion will run for more than 72 hours `o` Ketamine infusion does not need to be weaned - Stop infusion and disconnect when prescription discontinued 

THE NATIONAL HOSPITAL FOR NEUROLOGY AND NEUROSURGERY FEBRUARY 2024

The National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery Neuroscience Critical Care Unit (NCCU) Management of Sodium and Water Balance **HYPONATRAEMIA** (Na+ <135mmol/L) **CONSIDER TREATING SERUM SODIUM ≤130mmol/L** 

Normal plasma sodium =135–145mmo/L & plasma osmolality 285–295mOsm/kg 

Sodium disturbances are common after brain injury and treatment is indicated when there are acute symptoms that pose a risk of significant neurological complications. 

**CSW SIADH DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION** Serum sodium Low  (<135mmol/L) Low (<135mmol/L) [| Exclude normal or elevated P osm Serum osmolality Normal or increased Decreased (<280mOsm/kg) [| Exclude hypotonic hyponatraemia Volume status Hypovolaemia Normo- or euvolaemia [| Check urine osm Urine sodium Very high (>40mmol/L) High (>25mmol/L) [| Assess volume status and urine Urine osmolality High or normal High (urine osmo > serum osmo) Na+ Haematocrit Hi h Normal g **SYNDROME OF INAPPROPRIATE CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS CEREBRAL SALT WASTING** (CSW) **ANTIDIURETIC HORMONE** Lethargy (SIADH) Headache _Develops at;_ Nausea / Vomiting **Na[+] 125-130mmol/L** Na+ 125-130mmol/L when ACUTE **Na[+] 125-130mmol/L** Irritability Na+ 120-125mmol/L when CHRONIC Muscle cramps / The primary treatment of CSW is Aim to **RESTRICT** intake of water weakness volume and sodium resuscitation : Hyporeflexia _Develops at_ **FLUID RESTRICTION d** ependent on Drowsiness Na+ 120-125 mmol/L when ACUTE Aim to **REPLACE** sodium and water individual patient requirements Confusion Na+ 110-120 mmol/L when CHRONIC with sodium containing **ISOTONIC FLUIDS** _Consider risk of fluid restriction in patients with SAH_ Seizures _assume CSW until sure of In SAH patient with low Na+_ Coma Na+ <115mmol/L _diagnosis of SIADH because of consequences of fluid restriction_ Death Presence and severity related to: Restriction to 1.5L/24h usually sufficient 1. Severity of derangement 2. Speed at which derangement develops ~~eo -+—~~ **Na[+] <125mmol/L HYPERTONIC SALINE** _(HTS)_ **1.8%** for **24-48 HOURS** preferably via central venous line to increase sodium concentration by maximum **8-10mmol in 24 hours** (Hourly rise not >2mmol/L unless Na+ consistently low) _1.8% saline can be given peripherally but IV site must be closely monitored – not recommended if treatment required for >48h_ I **RATE OF INFUSION** o The rate of infusion should be prescribed for each patient individually OSMOTIC DEMYELINATION SYNDROME o Set **hourly / daily target rise** in Na[[+]] Characterised by damage to regions of brain, o Hypertonic saline usually started at ~ **30-40mL/h** most commonly pontine white matter tracts, o Once daily target achieved hypertonic saline should be stopped after rapid correction of metabolic disturbances _Rapid increase or decrease can cause myelinolysis_ such as hyponatremia 

The symptoms and signs relate not only to the severity of the sodium disturbance but also to the rate of change of serum sodium concentration. 

**HYPONATRAEMIA** is defined as a serum sodium concentration of <135mmol/L and It usually develops between 2 and 7 days after injury and is associated with mortality increases of up to 60%. Hyponatraemia creates a gradient across the blood–brain barrier allowing entry of water into the brain and the development of cerebral oedema. The cause of the low serum sodium can be determined by the associated volume status. 

Both **CSW** and **SIADH** can occur following brain injury.  It is important to distinguish between the two as the treatment differs.   For CSW the patient is given fluids and sodium supplementation, while for SIADH the patient is fluid restricted unless the sodium is severe (<125mmol/L) and the patient has significant symptoms. 

CSW is mostly seen in SAH and the patient must venous line to increase sodium concentration by maximum **8-10mmol in 24** be volume repleted while treating the **hours** (Hourly rise not >2mmol/L unless Na+ consistently low) hyponatraemia – with isotonic saline for mild to _1.8% saline can be given peripherally but IV site must be closely monitored –_ moderate cases, hypertonic saline (1.8%) for _not recommended if treatment required for >48h_ moderate to severe cases.   In some cases the use I of fludrocortisone is also advocated. **RATE OF INFUSION** Following confirmation of SIADH, the aim is to o The rate of infusion should be prescribed for each patient individually restrict the intake of water.  Important drug-related o Set **hourly / daily target rise** in Na[[+]] causes include diuretics (particularly thiazides) and o Hypertonic saline usually started at ~ **30-40mL/h** . anticonvulsant drugs (particularly carbamazepine) o Once daily target achieved hypertonic saline should be stopped _Rapid increase or decrease can cause myelinolysis_ The rate of serum sodium improvement in both I CSW and SIADH should be gradual (8- **FLUDROCORTISONE** 10mmol/24h) in most circumstances to avoid a If hyponatraemia refractory to standard therapy complications associated with rapid osmolar shifts a FLUID BALANCE consider fludrocortisone **50mcg/tds for 4 days** (cerebral oedema and osmotic demyelination a SERUM SODIUM Thereafter wean every 48h: _50mcg/BD for 48h,_ syndrome). Severe hyponatraemia (irrespective of _then 50mcg/OD for 48h  - then stop_ a Consider slow sodium tablets the cause) may lead to neurological complications a 0 and requires prompt management with intense _BD urine / serum Na_ **MONITOR** ————_—. monitoring. _If Na[[+]]_ THE NATIONAL HOSPITAL FOR NEUROLOGY AND NEUROSURGERY JULY 2024

|||||| |---|---|---|---|---| |The National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery| |University College| |Neuroscience Critical Care Unit (NCCU)| |London|Hospitals| |ACUTE ISCHAEMIC STROKE (AIS)| |NHS Foundation|Trust| |AIS is characterised by|sudden loss of blood circulation to an area of brain, typically in a vascular territory, resulting in corresponding loss of neurologic| |function. Ischaemic stroke is caused by thrombotic or embolic occlusion of a cerebral artery and more common than haemorrhagic stroke. Aim of treatment| |is to achieve recanalization and reperfusion of ischaemic penumbra with IV thrombolysis and / or endovascular thrombectomy in appropriate patients.| |This protocol outlines safe targets based on agreed guidelines, which may be adapted in individual clinical circumstances with the judgement / knowledge of the responsible senior clinician| |A.|Intubate|(if required)| |NEUROPROTECTIVE MEASURES|AIS DIAGNOSIS| |*| |B.|Ventilate: Pa02 >11-13kPa / PaCO2 4.5-5.5kPa 2 >11-13kPa / PaCO2 4.5-5.5kPa >11-13kPa / PaCO2 4.5-5.5kPa 2 4.5-5.5kPa 4.5-5.5kPa *| |Standard neuro|rotective measures should be undertaken| |p| |CONFIRMED| |Pa02 11 – 13kPa| |SEDATION: Propofol -|C.|IV access, fluids (0.9% saline), FBC, U&&Es, clotting, G&S,| |0.5mg/kg test bolus dose,|PaCO2 4.5 – 5.5kPa|A B C D E|BM / arterial line / acute BP control ((| |20-75mcg/kg/min infusion|►|Hypotension & hypovolaemia corrected to maintain systemic perfusion| |[[st]]|week|Head up to 30 degrees +|RESUSCITATION| |ANALGESIA: Fentanyl -|but no recommendation on lowest BP level required for good outcome| |neck straight| |day. However, neurological deterioration may occur|2mcg/kg test bolus dose,|NEUROPROTECTIVE|D.|GCS / pupillary response / limb assessment| |Levetiracetam 1g IV if| |then 2-5mcg/kg/h infusion| |suspicion of seizure activity|MEASURES|E.|Urinary catheter / NG tube| **----- End of picture text -----**
## **DEFINITION** A. Intubate _(if required)_ **AIS DIAGNOSIS** B. Ventilate: Pa02 >11-13kPa / PaCO2 4.5-5.5kPa 2 >11-13kPa / PaCO2 4.5-5.5kPa >11-13kPa / PaCO2 4.5-5.5kPa 2 4.5-5.5kPa 4.5-5.5kPa * **CONFIRMED** C. IV access, fluids (0.9% saline), FBC, U&&Es, clotting, G&S, **A B C D E** BM / arterial line / acute BP control (( _See BP targets below)_ ► _Hypotension & hypovolaemia corrected to maintain systemic perfusion_ RESUSCITATION _but no recommendation on lowest BP level required for good outcome_ NEUROPROTECTIVE D. GCS / pupillary response / limb assessment MEASURES E. Urinary catheter / NG tube **MEDICAL MANAGEMENT** Thrombolysis / **M** echanical **T** hrombectomy (MT) _+/- Thrombolysis_ No Reperfusion Therapy ► **BP MANAGEMENT POST MT** ► **BP MANAGEMENT** Consider risk of worsening ischaemia vs risk of haemorrhage **INCOMPLETE RECANALIZATION SBP <180mmHg** AIS occurs due to a variety of underlying pathological processes and about 85% of all strokes are due to ischaemia. Most hemispheric infarctions reach maximal swelling after 3 to 5 days. Occlusion of middle cerebral artery (MCA) or one of its branches is one of most common causes of AIS. Space occupying oedema is leading cause of mortality in 1[[st]] week following stroke and clinical features usually manifest between 2[nd] and 4[th] day. However, neurological deterioration may occur more rapidly within 2h of symptom onset, and when associated with involvement of the whole MCA territory has been termed ‘ . malignant MCA syndrome’ ## **SURGERY** : Haemorrhagic transformation is a common complication in patients with AIS. It occurs when peripheral blood extravasates across a disrupted blood brain barrier (BBB) into the brain following ischemic stroke – _factors linked to increased risk include reperfusion therapy (thrombolysis / thrombectomy), hypertension._ **CURRENT AIS GUIDELINES** https://www.strokeguideline.org/app/uploads/2023/04/National-ClinicalGuideline-for-Stroke-2023.pdf https://doi.org/10.1161/STR.0000000000000211 _**IMAGING**_ 1. Non-contrast brain CT – to exclude haemorrhagic stroke 2. MRI once patient stabilised (ideally within first 24 hours) _**TREATMENT**_ Acute management aim is to prevent infarction and minimise permanent brain damage. Early treatment is critical to rescue potentially salvageable tissue (‘time is brain’) with effective arterial recanalization to restore blood flow. Patients who present <4.5 hours after stroke onset and have no contraindications may be considered for thrombolysis _(see below_ ). Patients who are not eligible for thrombolysis (due to delayed time to presentation or contraindications to thrombolysis) can be considered for endovascular therapy _(see_ . _below)_ EVD for hydrocephalus / Posterior Fossa Decompression / Decompressive Craniectomy ► **BP MANAGEMENT POST-OP** **INCOMPLETE RECANALIZATION SBP <180mmHg SBP <180mmHg** to maintain adequate blood flow to ischaemic bed and reduce risk of infarct​ion **FULL RECANALIZATION​ SBP <160mmHg** to minimise risk of reperfusion injury **HAEMORRHAGIC TRANSFORMATION SBP <160mmHg** for **48-72h** to minimise risk of further bleeding - _then consider relaxing to <180mmHg_ **BP LOWERING DRUGS** 1[st] 24-48 HOURS _1. Labetalol infusion (1[st] line) – see protocol 2._ VENTILATION _Hydralazine infusion (2[nd] line) – see protocol 3. Oral / NG antihypertensive treatment started &_ SEDATION / ANALGESIA _IV therapy weaned / stopped within 2-3 days_ +/- ICP MONITORING ►IF ICP MONITORED: ICP <22mmHg Maintain Glucose 7-12mmol/L ∼ ► Treat Hypoglycaemia CPP 60mmHg ► Treat Hyperglycaemia _(Range: 50-70mmHg)_ ► Treat temperature >37.5[o] C with antipyretic **Class** of **Recommendation** ► Consider **T** argeted **T** emperature **M** anagement ► **Class 1: STRONG** _Recommended (Benefit >>> Risk)_ **WEANING SEDATION** ► **Class 2a: MODERATE** Consider weaning sedation after 24-48h _Reasonable (Benefit >> Risk)_ ► **Class 2b: WEAK** _but only once BP under control / May be reasonable_ (Benefit ≥ Risk) _consistently meeting target_ ► **Class 3: STRONG** _Potential harm (Risk > Benefit)_ **==> picture [393 x 458] intentionally omitted <==** **----- Start of picture text -----**
SBP <160mmHg for 1 [st] 24-48h
To reduce risk of post-op bleeding
Thereafter revert to <180mmHg
1 [st] 24-48 HOURS
VENTILATION
SEDATION / ANALGESIA
+/- ICP MONITORING
►IF ICP MONITORED:
ICP <22mmHg
∼
CPP 60mmHg
(Range: 50-70mmHg)
**----- End of picture text -----**
_BP management_ : Lowering BP may reduce risk of haemorrhagic transformation and oedema, whereas high BP may protect brain by maintaining cerebral perfusion when autoregulation impaired. BP targets reflect risk of worsening ischaemia versus risk of haemorrhage – _patient specific BP decisions also based on imaging._ **==> picture [353 x 110] intentionally omitted <==** **----- Start of picture text -----**
Post decompressive craniectomy
•
ICP target may be revised
•
DO NOT lie patient on side of
decompression
**----- End of picture text -----**
_Thrombol_ ysis: IV injection of recombinant tissue plasminogen activator (r-tPA) is gold standard treatment for ischaemic stroke, given within 4.5 hours following onset of symptoms if agreed criteria met ( _extended to 4.5-9 hours for ‘wake-up’ stroke)._ *Self-ventilating patients ► Supplemental oxygen to maintain SaO2 >94% ► Supplemental oxygen not recommended in non-hypoxic patients with AIS : _Mechanical Thrombectomy (MT)_ intra-arterial thrombolysis or . mechanical clot retrieval in eligible patients 6-16h after stroke _Decompressive hemicraniectomy_ : for malignant MCA infarct should be considered (within 48 hours of symptom onset) for people with acute stroke who meet agreed criteria. THE NATIONAL HOSPITAL FOR NEUROLOGY AND NEUROSURGERY JULY 2024

text:
The National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery Neuroscience Critical Care Unit (NCCU) **4. DIAGNOSING DEATH** by **NEUROLOGICAL CRITERIA (DNC) – TEST PROCEDURE** Academy of Medical Royal Colleges (2025) Code of Practice in UK - _‘When correctly applied, neurological criteria unequivocally diagnose death, confirming the permanent loss of the capacity for consciousness combined_ - _with permanent loss of the capacity to breathe . Certainty that the cessation_ - _of brainstem function is permanent is assured by satisfying the criteria below.’_ ## **TIMING OF TESTS** Time **before testing** should equal time taken to meet essential preconditions and necessary exclusions **- PRECONDITIONS** and **EXCLUSIONS** must be met before tests are carried out When decision made that brainstem tests will be completed, Specialist Nurse for Organ Donation (SNOD) should be contacted via 24 hour **03000 20 30 40** pager: ## **WHO CAN DO THE TESTS** UK Code of Practice requires **two doctors** with expertise in the field to make the diagnosis of brainstem death - Senior clinicians (registered >5 years) with interest in intensive care medicine / anaesthesia - One practitioner a consultant, the other consultant / senior registrar - `o` Testing should be undertaken by both doctors together - `o` All parts of 1[st] & 2[nd] set of tests completed before brainstem death can be confirmed The two doctors work together to perform full set of clinical tests. Tests are then repeated and must always be performed on two occasions i.e. total of 2 sets of clinical tests (including 2 apnoea tests) performed. ## **1[st] set of tests** : ## _**DOCTOR A** completes set of clinical tests whilst Doctor B observes_ ## **2[nd] set of tests:** - _**DOCTOR B** completes set of clinical tests whilst Doctor A observes_ - _actual tests only performed twice_ If 1[st] set of clinical tests shows no evidence of brainstem function, there need not be a lengthy delay before performing 2[nd] set. Appropriate to use time between tests to inform family of result and allow family members to attend 2[nd] set of clinical tests, if desired. The patient's arterial blood gases and baseline parameters should be restored to pre-test level, before commencing 2[nd] set of tests. ## **TIME OF DEATH** Time of death = time of **2[nd] set of tests** (or time of ancillary testing if after 2[nd] set of tests) |**TESTS FOR THE ABSENCE OF BRAINSTEM FUNCTION**

text:
The National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery Neuroscience Critical Care Unit (NCCU) 

## **CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM (CNS) INFECTION** 

CNS infections, meningitis, encephalitis, brain abscess, are rare but time-sensitive emergencies – certain features are common to all but speed at which they develop, and intensity varies depending on causative organism – mortality and morbidity are significant. 

**MENINGITIS** Is a regional infection affecting the meninges, usually the arachnoid and pia mater, and the intervening space.  Signs and symptoms include fever, headache, neck stiffness, non-blanching haemorrhagic rash in meningococcal meningitis. _Bacterial meningitis_ occurs when organisms gain access to subarachnoid either space through bacteraemia (from upper airway source), contiguous spread from dental or sinus infections, traumatic or congenital communications with exterior, or a neurosurgical procedure. The severe inflammation associated with bacterial meningitis results in oedema of brain and meninges, and eventually increased ICP once compensatory mechanisms for CSF displacement overwhelmed. Bacterial meningitis is associated with significant morbidity and mortality. Acute bacterial meningitis is a neurological emergency and clinical outcome dependent on immediate antibiotic therapy _Viral Meningitis_ has no specific anti-viral therapy for most causes and treatment is largely supportive with spontaneous recovery anticipated in most cases. _Fungal Meningitis_ is almost always a disease of the immunocompromised. **ENCEPHALITIS** is a diffuse infection affecting brain tissue +/- inflammation of the meninges. Signs and symptoms include altered mental state (confusion, drowsiness), seizures, changes in personality and behaviour. It is a life-threatening condition with high mortality (as high as 70% if left untreated) and morbidity rates. Early identification and treatment is essential. 

**BRAIN ABSCESS** is a localised area of necrosis with a surrounding membrane within the brain parenchyma, usually from an infectious or traumatic process. The most frequent microbial pathogens isolated are Staphylococcus / Streptococcus. Signs and symptoms include focal neurology, seizures headache, nausea, vomiting. 

SEE SPECIFIC GUIDELINES FOR NEUROSURGICAL INFECTIONS 

**TESTS** and **INVESTIGATIONS –** _**BUT DO NOT DELAY STARTING TREATMENT**_ All meningitis patients should be isolated for 24 hours unless meningococcal disease excluded j Use Epic ‘ **ADULT MENINGITIS PANEL** ’ to ensure all recommended investigations sent (type ‘Meningitis’ into Orders) 

- Blood cultures before antibiotics prior to broad spectrum antibiotic / antiviral therapy wherever possible and WITHIN 1 HOUR OF PRESENTATION 

- `o` Venous blood gas (or consider arterial if hypoxic) 

- `o` FBC, U&E, LFTs, CRP, Clotting, Glucose 

- `o` Blood for pneumococcal and meningococcal PCR (purple top EDTA) • 

- `o` HIV test (purple top EDTA) If already on PROPHYLACTIC LMWH, LP should not be performed until 12h after dose • 

- `o` Prophylactic LMWH should be delayed until 4h after LP performed Serum save (gold top) • 

- | Patients on THERAPEUTIC LMWH should not have LP until 24h after dose • 

- CT scan _(If CT shows brain abscess with mass effect surgery essential)_ Patients on warfarin – LP should not be performed until INR is <1.4 • Patients on aspirin and other NSAIDs do not need LP delayed 

- • 

- Lumbar puncture safe if: LP in PATIENTS on ANTICOAGULANTS Patients on clopidogrel and DOACs (e.g. rivaroxaban, dabigatran) - LP delayed for 7 ✓ _not in coma_ days - discuss with haematology • 

- ✓ _no focal neurological signs_ Patients with known thrombocytopenia - LP should not be performed at platelet counts of <40 x 109/L or with a rapidly falling platelet count 

- ✓ _no papilloedema_ • LP should not be delayed for results of blood tests unless high clinical suspicion of 

- CSF analysis _– for subsequent definitive treatment_ bleeding diathesis 

- **START ANTIBIOTIC + ANTIVIRAL TREATMENT AS SOON AS POSSIBLE SEE UCLH GUIDELINES** 

**START ANTIBIOTIC + ANTIVIRAL TREATMENT AS SOON AS POSSIBLE 1[st] line: Ceftriaxone 2g IV BD + Aciclovir 10mg/kg** _(IBW if obese)_ **TDS** If **BACTERIAL** cause suspected: ADD Dexamethasone 10mg IV qds - 1[st] dose given just before, with, or up to 12h after 1[st] dose of antibiotics 

**MENINGITIS –** _Regional infection_ **ENCEPHALITIS –** _Diffuse infection_ Infection affects meninges, usually arachnoid, pia, and intervening space brain tissue +/- inflammation of meninges MENINGOCOCCAL PNEUMOCOCCAL TB VIRAL HSV-1 / VZV / Ceftriaxone or Cefotaxime Ceftriaxone or Cefotaxime Anti-TB drugs ENTEROVIRUS BACTERIAL ENTEROVIRUS 2g IV BD for at least 5 days 2g IV BD for 10-14 days _isoniazid / rifampin /_ & HSV-2 _pyrazinamide / + either_ STEROIDS STEROIDS _streptomycin or ethambutol_ Appropriate If pneumococcal meningitis Symptomatic Anti-viral therapy antibiotic If not pneumococcal meningitis, confirmed, or thought probable Steroids may be indicated relief only and ACICLOVIR _Most common_ stop dexamethasone _(based on clinical, epidemiological Anti TB drugs only given when_ supportive 10mg/kg _(IBW if cause Listeria and CSF parameters),_ continue _definitive diagnosis of TB made_ therapy _obese)_ for 21 days _- Treat with_ dexamethasone for 4 days _or after excluding other causes amoxicillin_ Consider areas with known risk of _in comatose patients since_ resistance (USA, South Africa, _once started these drugs must_ Spain) - may need vancomycin _be iven for a lon time g g_ Close contacts in 2 days prior to admission should be Screen for primary Screen for primary focus of screened and given **BRAIN ABSCESS –** _Focal infection_ source of infection TB rifampicin for 2 days Intracerebral or subdural empyema Possibly also staff involved Surgery for lesions >2.5cm in intubating patient Antibiotics Infection precautions MEROPENEM 2g IV tds Infection precautions ( _review within 72h with culture results) Droplet propagated Infected patients require_ Abx treatment usually for 6-8 weeks _Normal precautions after 1[st] isolation precautions for 2 dose of antibiotics given_ Steroids _weeks from start of treatment Use visor and closed suction_

The National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery Neuroscience Critical Care Unit (NCCU) ► **Brain Tissue Oxygen** (PbtO2) **Guided Management** of _Treated a_ **Aneurysmal Subarachnoid Haemorrhage** ( SAH) Monitoring of brain tissue oxygenation is an important part of multimodality monitoring (MMM). Continuous monitoring of PbtO2 is an additional tool to enable detection of impaired cerebral blood flow after SAH – it allows the early identification of patients who are at increased risk of delayed cerebral ischaemia (DCI), using medical management by manipulating SBP and O2 and timely radiological intervention to help prevent infarction. It can help identify the need for and the effect of various therapies such as hyperoxia, induced hypertension and targeted temperature management in SAH patients. **==> picture [2180 x 1318] intentionally omitted <==** **----- Start of picture text -----**

text:
## The National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery Neuroscience Critical Care Unit (NCCU) _**a**_ **ANEURYSMAL SUBARACHNOID HAEMORRHAGE ( SAH)** Protocol based on agreed guidelines, which may be adapted in individual clinical circumstances with judgement / knowledge of responsible senior clinician ## **DEFINITION** Subarachnoid haemorrrhage (SAH) is defined as bleeding into the subarachnoid space - therefore into the CSF. Cerebral arteries lie in subarachnoid space and give off small perforating branches to brain tissue. Bleeding from these vessels or from an associated aneurysm occurs primarily into this space. Some intracranial aneurysms are embedded within the brain tissue and their rupture causes intracerebral bleeding with or without SAH. Occasionally, the arachnoid layer is disrupted and a subdural haematoma results. Following SAH, blood within the CSF can disrupt the reabsorption of CSF causing secondary hydrocephalus. The leading cause of SAH is rupture of an intracranial _a_ saccular aneurysm ( SAH), a dilatation in the wall of an artery, usually at the junction or bifurcation of arteries. Other causes include anticoagulants, hypertension, arteriovenous malformation (AVM), bleeding diathesis, neoplasm and idiopathic. ## **CURRENT SAH GUIDELINES** https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/STR.0000000000000436# ## _**IMAGING**_ 1. Non-contrast brain CT – _if negative for aSAH, lumbar puncture (LP) to diagnose / exclude SAH_ 2. If negative or inconclusive, CT angiography (CTA), digital subtraction angiography (DSA) is indicated to diagnose / exclude cerebral aneurysm 3. DSA is considered the gold-standard investigation and can aid decision-making on optimal treatment ## _**MANAGEMENT TO PREVENT REBLEEDING**_ - BP should be controlled using short acting medication to prevent severe hypotension, hypertension, and BP variability – aim BP <160mmHg - Anticoagulant therapy stopped immediately and reversed - ► Surgical / endovascular treatment of aneurysm as early as possible following presentation, preferably within 24h of onset, to improve outcome – _to reduce risk of rebleeding and facilitate management of delayed cerebral ischaemia (DCI) (see protocol)_ **==> picture [79 x 13] intentionally omitted <==** **----- Start of picture text -----**
Circle of Willis
**----- End of picture text -----**
**==> picture [158 x 13] intentionally omitted <==** **----- Start of picture text -----**
Classic ’star fish’ sign of SAH
**----- End of picture text -----**
## **MANAGEMENT on ADMISSION to NCCU** **Nimodipine 60mg / 4 hourly NG** _or_ **PO** ## **CLINICAL ASSESSMENT** _Early enteral nimodipine beneficial in preventing DCI and improving outcome. Consistent administration recommended even in cases of nimodipine-induced hypotension_ **Neurology** _**GCS, Pupils, Limbs**_ - Commence as soon as SAH diagnosed - Continue for 21 days - 1 hourly for 4h - If hypotensive after dose, change to 30mg / 2 hourly - ► If not absorbing, give IV (1-2mg/h) via dedicated CVC lumen _(to run concurrently with 0.9% saline @ 40mL/h)_ - 2 hourly thereafter if stable - 1 hourly pupil assessment if patient sedated - _(Additional monitoring may be needed when neuro exam not possible, as in high grade aSAH)_ - **Intravascular Volume** and **Electrolyte Management** - Aim for **EUVOLAEMIA** (2-3L/24h adequate for most) - ► **Hypervolaemia** potentially harmful - _association with excess morbidity_ ## **Vital signs** _**Heart Rate**_ - Continuous recording of HR / rhythm - Use of mineralocorticoids is reasonable to treat natriuresis - 12 Lead ECG on admission and daily while QT prolonged - ► Avoid drugs that prolong QT interval - and hyponatraemia _(see protocol)_ ## **Glycaemic control** - - _**Blood Pressure** Lowering of BP to reduce risk of rebleeding versus risk of secondary ischaemia_ - Aim glucose 7-12mmol/L _(see protocol)_ - Avoid hypotension / hypertension / BP variability if possible unsecured aneurysm – _avoid hypertensive peaks_ - Strict hyperglycaemia management, and avoidance of hypoglycaemia may improve outcome ► Cautious treatment of hypertension – Aim SBP ≤160mmHg _(range: 120-160mmHg)_ **Venous thromboembolism** (VTE) **Prophylaxis** - Hypotension _(SBP <120mmHg)_ – _initially treat with IV crystalloid_ - Intermittent pneumatic compression device +/- TEDs - ► Withhold LMWH if aneurysm unprotected - Sustained hypertension _(SBP >160mmHg) – reduction in BP may be achieved with_ - _analgesics and nimodipine, but if not, consider short-acting medication (IV infusion of labetalol)_ - 24h after aneurysm secured, pharmacological prophylaxis recommended to reduce risk for VTE - _**Respiratory**_ - Continuous monitoring of SpO2 - LMWH should be withheld 24h before and 24h after any subsequent intracranial procedures - Oxygen therapy if SpO2 <95% - If mechanical ventilation for >24h, standardised ICU care bundle recommended to ## **Analgesia / Anti-emetic** - reduce duration of mechanical ventilation / HAP _- lung-protection / low tidal volume ventilation,_ - _early enteral nutrition, standardised antibiotic therapy for HAP, and systematic approach to extubation_ - NSAIDs not recommended for analgesia prior to treating aneurysm, but consider once aneurysm treated - ► Cyclizine for nausea - In patients who develop severe acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) and lifethreatening hypoxaemia, rescue manoeuvres such as prone positioning and alveolar recruitment may be reasonable to improve oxygenation - Avoid ondansetron due to susceptibility to QT interval prolongation ## _**Temperature**_ **Seizure Management –** Anti-epileptic Drugs (AEDs) ► Aim normothermia - target temperature 37.0C±0.5C - **Routine** prophylactic AEDs not recommended - Targeted Temperature Management (TTM) implemented with fever refractory to - Prophylactic AEDs may be reasonable if **high seizure** - - **risk features** _Ruptured MCA aneurysm, high-grade SAH, ICH,_ - _hydrocephalus, cortical infarct_ - antipyretic medications during acute phase - _TTM should be initiated as rapidly as possible once fever detected if pharmacological treatment has not controlled temperature_ _**within 1h** of administration - 1[st] line paracetamol / 1g / qds (see protocol)_ - Treatment with AEDs for ≤7 days is reasonable in patients **presenting with seizures** **Class** of **Recommendation** ► **Class 1: STRONG** - Full – _with full_ **clerking** prior to angiography _**neurological**_ - Treatment with AEDs for >7days not beneficial _**examination** if possible_ - Phenytoin **not** recommended for prophylaxis - _Due to associated cognitive effects and poor outcome_ - Patient should be on **bed rest** _Recommended (Benefit >>> Risk)_ ► **Class 2a: MODERATE** _Reasonable (Benefit >> Risk)_ ► **Class 2b: WEAK** _May be reasonable_ (Benefit ≥ Risk) ► **Class 3: STRONG** _Potential harm (Risk > Benefit)_ - Surgical team **book angiography** - Other AEDs can be considered - _If so, short course of 3-7 days_ - _Levetiracetam (Keppra) recommended_ - ► Local practice should determine duration of AEDs – _but should be reviewed at 3 months_ - **Routine bloods i** ncluding **eGFR** - **16G cannula** (grey) required - Angiography +/- proceed to definitive treatment within 24h of admission to NHNN THE NATIONAL HOSPITAL FOR NEUROLOGY AND NEUROSURGERY OCTOBER 2024
