text:
University College London Hospitals 

Management of Spinal Neurogenic Bladder & Bowel 

- AD is a potentially dangerous complication for patients with a spinal cord injury (SCI) at T6 or above. In AD, an individual's blood pressure may rise to dangerous levels and if not treated can lead to stroke and possibly death.  AD usually occurs when there is a noxious (irritating) stimulus below the level of the injury. Bladder distention, blocked catheters and urinary tract infections are frequent causes of AD. Constipation, poorly managed bowel care and digital removal of faeces can be causes of AD. Symptoms include headache, tachycardia or bradycardia, facial flush, perspiration, and a stuffy nose (United Spinal Association, 2015). 

- It is imperative that patients with an established cord injury are able to continue their normal bladder and bowel management, including trans anal irrigation.  Patients may wish to have their own personal carers perform their bladder/bowel regime while inpatients,  including manual evacuation. If patients chose to have their own careers this should be  facilitated by the ward and be part of the patients care plan. 

The care of patients with spinal neurogenic SCI is supported by the following documents and resources: 

1. Acute spinal cord injury (ASCI) bladder management Flow diagram that simplifies the specific care pathways that the patient needs for optimal treatment following a diagnosis of neurogenic bladder(appendix 1) 

2. ASCI bowel management Flow diagram that simplifies the specific care pathways that the patient needs for optimal treatment following a diagnosis of neurogenic bowel (appendix 2) 

3. A comprehensive bundle that outlines the best evidence based care - ‘Bladder care in acute spinal cord injury’ (appendix 3) 

4. A comprehensive bundle that outlines the best evidence based care - ‘Bowel care in acute spinal cord injury’ (appendix 4) 

5. Bristol stool chart (appendix 5) 

6. ASIA assessment chart (appendix 6) 

7. Patient safety alert Resources to support safer bowel care for patients at risk of autonomic dysreflexia (appendix 7)

image_path: /content/page_images/Management of Spinal Neurogenic Bladder and Bowel.pdf_p18.jpg
text:
University College London Hospitals 

Management of Spinal Neurogenic Bladder & Bowel 

## **Appendix 3** 

## **BLADDER CARE IN ACUTE SPINAL CORD INJURY (ASCI)** 

Patients with spinal cord injury (SCI) will be left with a neurogenic bladder, caused by neurological damage to the spinal cord.  Bladder dysfunction is broadly divided into Reflexic (spastic) and Areflexic (flaccid) or Mixed (combination of flaccid / spastic).  The type and symptoms will depend on the level and whether there is any motor / sensory function preservation of the sacral nerves (complete versus incomplete SCI) 

## **Reflexic (spastic)** 

Patients with a Neurological Level of Injury (NLI) at T12 and above, are likely to have a reflexic bladder function. 

These patients experience involuntary bladder contractions and feel the need to urinate even when there is little or no urine in the bladder.  Bladder (detrusor) contractions are typically poorly coordinated with the external sphincter and involuntary contractions occur (Shenot, 2016). 

## **Symptoms include:** 

Frequency, urgency, nocturia, spastic paralysis with sensory deficits, urine leakage (Shenot, 2016) and sexual dysfunction and erectile dysfunction in men, pelvic pain, bladder over-extension. These symptoms can vary by site and severity of the lesion and fall into two categories: 

## • **Detrusor Sphincter Dysynergia (DSD)** 

- A lack of coordination between contraction and relaxation of the detrusor muscle and external sphincter. Both contract at the same time causing high intravesical pressure, incomplete voiding, and risk of vesico-ureteric reflux (Ontario Neurotrauma Foundation and Department of Family Medicine, 2012). 

## • **Neurogenic Detrusor Overactivity (NDO)** 

- An increased activity of the detrusor muscle usually combined with impaired contractility and a lack of coordination between the sphincter and the detrusor. The detrusor contracts at small volumes, and against an overactive sphincter (Middleton et al., 2013). 

All reflexic patients are at high risk of vesico-urethral reflux (due to the high bladder pressures) which may result in recurrent bladder infections, bladder stones, hydronephrosis, pyelonephritis, and renal failure (Middleton et al.,2013). 

## **Initial Treatment** 

UCLH - 2019 Published (16/04/2019) Date: Review Date: (12/03/2022) Policies, procedures and guidelines only current on date printed. Refer to the intranet for definitive version

image_path: /content/page_images/Management of Spinal Neurogenic Bladder and Bowel.pdf_p19.jpg
text:
University College London Hospitals 

Management of Spinal Neurogenic Bladder & Bowel Immediate, continuous catheterization is needed.  If the patient’s catheter is bypassing, consideration of the use of an anticholinergic/alpha-blocker drug, as this may ease the spasms preventing urine bypassing. 

Bladder training can commence as soon as appropriate by clamping off the catheter and asking the patient to tell you when they feel the need to pass urine.  NB Do not leave the catheter clamped for more than 4 hours without draining it. 

As soon as possible, patient should be trialed without a catheter (TWOC) (NICE, 2014). If the patient manages to pass urine, ward staff must record 2448 hours worth of pre and post void bladder scans, along with the volume of urine passed.  If post void volumes are 20% or more of the pre void volume, the patient is likely to need intermittent self-catheterization (ISC) training to empty bladder completely. 

Note should also be taken if a patient passes small amounts frequently (this denotes that the patient may need an anticholenergic /alpha-blocker drug). They must be seen by uro-neurology (NHNN), urology (Tower) as soon as possible as an inpatient. 

Intermittent self-catheterization is preferable to indwelling urethral catheterization, which has a high risk of recurrent UTIs and in men, a high risk of urethritis, periurethritis, prostatic abscesses, and urethral fistulas. Suprapubic catheterization may be used if patients cannot self-catheterize (see appendix A for acute spinal cord bladder management pathway). 

If patients fail TWOC, it is ideal for them to be taught ISC straight away.  If this is not possible, the re-insertion of a long term indwelling catheter is needed, and early consideration given to a supra pubic catheter. 

## **Areflexic (Flaccid) Bladder** 

Patients with a cord injury below NLI T12 are likely to have an areflexic (flaccid), bladder function.  These patients are likely to experience little or no contraction of the detrusor muscle. This causes urine retention with no sensation of a filling or full bladder (painless retention of urine), until the urine overflows.  Bladder volumes are large with a low pressure and contractions are absent (Dorsher and McIntosh, 2012) 

## **Symptoms include:** 

Urinary retention, overflow incontinence, sexual dysfunction and erectile dysfunction in men, pelvic pain, bladder over-extension, autonomic dysreflexia (Shenot, 2016). 

## **Initial Treatment**

image_path: /content/page_images/Management of Spinal Neurogenic Bladder and Bowel.pdf_p20.jpg
text:
University College London Hospitals 

Management of Spinal Neurogenic Bladder & Bowel Initial treatment consists of immediate, continuous catherization.  As soon as possible, the patient should be trialed without a catheter (NICE, 2014).  If the patient manages to pass urine, ward staff must record 24-48 hours’ worth of pre and post void bladder scans, along with the volume of urine passed.  If post void volumes are 20% or more of the pre void volume, the patient is likely to need intermittent catheterization training to empty bladder completely. They must be seen by uro-neurology (NHNN) / urology (Tower) ASAP as an inpatient. 

Intermittent self-catheterization is preferable to indwelling urethral catheterization, which has a high risk of recurrent UTIs and in men, a high risk of urethritis, periurethritis, prostatic abscesses, and urethral fistulas. Suprapubic catheterization may be used if patients cannot self-catheterize (see appendix 1 for acute spinal cord bladder management pathway). 

If patients fail TWOC, it is ideal for them to be taught ISC straight away.  If this is not possible, the re-insertion of a long term indwelling catheter is needed and early consideration of a supra pubic catheter. 

## **On-going treatment for all SCI Patients** 

All SCI patients with bladder involvement should be referred on to UroNeurology.  This can happen as an outpatient.  They will need further diagnosis and on-going treatment, which may involve imaging, teaching other manoeuvres, (e.g. Valsalva) cystoscopy, urodynamic testing and possibly surgery (NICE, 2012). 

## **Mixed Pattern** 

A mixed pattern may occur if the injury is at the conus terminale, (where the spinal cord finishes and the cauda equina begins) around T11-L2 resulting in a flaccid **or** spastic bladder.  These patients can experience a combination of symptoms from both reflexic and areflexic bladders. 

**It is important to note that acutely after cord injury, spinal shock occurs, where all bladders are flaccid generally for up to 2 weeks (but may be longer or much shorter), and after this time as the cord recovers, the more permanent clinical picture will arise.** 

## **Additional Notes** 

Autonomic Dysreflexia (AD) is a potentially dangerous complication for patients with a spinal cord injury (SCI) at T6 or above. In AD, an individual's blood pressure may rise to dangerous levels and if not treated can lead to stroke and possibly death.  AD usually occurs when there is a noxious (irritating) stimulus below the level of the injury. Bladder distention and UTI’s are frequent causes of AD.  Symptoms include headache, tachycardia or bradycardia, facial flush, perspiration, and a stuffy nose (United Spinal Association, 2015). 

UCLH - 2019 Published (16/04/2019) Date: Review Date: (12/03/2022) Policies, procedures and guidelines only current on date printed. Refer to the intranet for definitive version

text:
University College London Hospitals 

Management of Spinal Neurogenic Bladder & Bowel 

## **Appendix 4** 

## **BOWEL CARE IN ACUTE SPINAL CORD INJURY (ASCI)** 

Patients with Spinal cord injury (SCI) will be left with a neurogenic bowel, caused by neurological damage to the spinal cord. Neurogenic bowel dysfunction denotes impaired sensory and / or motor control of the anorectum, resulting in reduced or absent voluntary control of defecation.  Stool transit through the bowel, therefore, may be very slow. Patients are at high risk of complications ranging from severe constipation to bowel perforation. The combination of both constipation and impaired continence is termed neurogenic bowel dysfunction (Ash, 2005). 

Emmanuel (2010) and Coggrave& Norton (2013), point out the major negative impact poor bowel management has on a patient’s quality of life, being the second most distressing aspect of injury after loss of mobility.  It inhibits the ability to socialize, live independently, impacts on sexuality and is amongst the most restrictive consequences of their injury.  It is imperative that effective evidence based management begins immediately. 

A neurogenic bowel describes colon dysfunction due to loss of normal sensory and motor control as a result of neurological damage (Chung and Emmanuel, 2006). The neurological level of injury (NLI) determines neurogenic bowel behavior.  This is divided into: Reflexic (spastic), Areflexic (flaccid) and mixed pattern. 

## **Reflexic (spastic)** 

Patients with a NLI above the conus medullaris (usually T12) are likely to have an upper motor neurone (Reflexic) bowel.  Patients have intact reflex arcs from the conus medullaris, characterized by increased colonic wall and anal tones.  This allows patients to use the maintained tone to aid movement of faeces through the colon and defaecate. Studies show (Krassioukov, Janice et al (2010) that, voluntary control of the external anal sphincter is disrupted and the sphincter remains tight, thereby promoting stool retention. 

UCLH - 2019 Published (16/04/2019) Date: Review Date: (12/03/2022) Policies, procedures and guidelines only current on date printed. Refer to the intranet for definitive version

image_path: /content/page_images/Management of Spinal Neurogenic Bladder and Bowel.pdf_p23.jpg
text:
University College London Hospitals 

Management of Spinal Neurogenic Bladder & Bowel Reflexic bowels are typically associated with constipation and faecal retention; 

at least in part due to external anal sphincter over activity (dis-coordinated sphincters). 

## **Symptoms include:** 

Loss / impaired sensory perception of the need to defaecate 

Impaired voluntary contraction of anal sphincter 

Tone is increased and modulation of peristalsis from the brain is lost causing uncoordinated spasms. 

Transit time of faeces through the colon is increased 

Constipation / Faecal impaction 

Overflow incontinence 

Haemorrhoids 

Less common - Faecal incontinence (MASCIP, 2012) 

## **Initial Treatment** 

According to current MASCIP clinical guidelines (2012) a bowel management plan should be instigated within 24 hours of an injury to the cord. 

At UCLH it is imperative that our local policy is followed: 

Establishing a daily routine at the same time is key to gaining control over continence and avoiding a build-up of stool in the colon.  It is best to establish this in the morning when the gastro colic reflex is strongest (This is a physiological response that occurs when one eats.  The stretch receptors in the stomach initiate gut motility and peristalsis within the GI tract hence aiding defecation). 

Regular oral slow acting laxatives should be given at night (e.g. Sennakot, 2 tablets) and faster acting in the morning with breakfast e.g. Macrogol, to achieve a Bristol stool (Lewis & Heaton, 1997) consistency of 4. 

The ward pharmacist should be made aware that the patient is following a regime and the drugs should be reviewed at 72 hours and titrated to each individual’s needs. 

UCLH - 2019 Published (16/04/2019) Date: Review Date: (12/03/2022) Policies, procedures and guidelines only current on date printed. Refer to the intranet for definitive version

image_path: /content/page_images/Management of Spinal Neurogenic Bladder and Bowel.pdf_p24.jpg
text:
University College London Hospitals 

Management of Spinal Neurogenic Bladder & Bowel 

Patient should be given their breakfast and a **HOT** drink. This is to stimulate the gastrocolic reflex.  Patients need to be educated on diet.  Generally a diet containing 5 portions of fruit / veg and increased levels of wholegrain should be adhered to.  Good breakfast suggestions are Weetabix, bran flakes, brown bread, fruit juice and a hot drink.  Patients, from a bowel point of view, should be encouraged to have an increased level of fluid, but bladder management needs to be taken into account here. 

Gentle rectal stimulant should be inserted after breakfast (e.g. Glycerine suppository 4g x 2).  This helps to trigger evacuation of stool; oral laxatives alone will not achieve this. 

The patient or staff member should then undertake abdominal massage.  This is firm but gentle massage of the abdomen in a clockwise direction using the heel of ones hand; starting in the bottom right hand corner, in a continuous motion up across and down the abdomen, following the usual lie of the colon. Studies show that abdominal massage produces a measurable response in the rectum and colon (Coggrave et al 2007). 

Digital rectal stimulation should occur around 20 minutes after suppositories are inserted, either at the bedside or over the toilet.  (See additional notes re RCN guidance). This technique is used to increase reflex muscular activity in the colon to aid stool propulsion. 

Once the patient has defecated, digital rectal examination of the rectum must occur to ensure the rectum is empty.  If it is not empty the nurse / patient should progress to digital removal of faeces and if required this cycle may need to occur again. (See additional notes re RCN for clarification of all qualified nurses responsibilities (RCN 2012). 

UCLH - 2019 Published (16/04/2019) Date: Review Date: (12/03/2022) Policies, procedures and guidelines only current on date printed. Refer to the intranet for definitive version

text:
University College London Hospitals 

Management of Spinal Neurogenic Bladder & Bowel 

## **Areflexic (Flaccid) Bowel** 

Patients with a NLI below the conus medullaris, (usually T12 or below) are likely to have an areflexic (flaccid) bowel function. They lose  centrallymediated spinal cord peristalsis; that is no reflex activity is spared in the colon, causing slow stool propulsion.  Areflexic bowels are commonly associated with constipation and a significant risk of incontinence due to the atonic (flaccid) external anal sphincter and lack of control over the pelvic floor and levator ani muscle that causes the lumen of the rectum to open (Krassioukov, Janice et al 2010). 

## **Symptoms include:** 

Loss / impaired sensory perception of the need to defaecate Nill or impaired voluntary contraction of anal sphincter 

No spinal cord peristalsis, loss of effective faecal transit. 

Flaccid external anal sphincter, pelvic floor 

Transit time of faeces through the colon is considerably increased 

Faecal incontinence 

Constipation / Faecal impaction 

Overflow incontinence Haemorrhoids (MASCIP, 2012) 

## **Initial Treatment** 

According to current MASCIP clinical guidelines (2012) a bowel management plan should be instigated within 24 hours. 

At UCLH it is imperative that our local policy is followed: 

Establishing a daily routine at the same time is key to gaining control over continence and avoiding a build-up of stool in the colon.  It is best to establish this in the morning when the gastro colic reflex is strongest (This is a

text:
University College London Hospitals 

Management of Spinal Neurogenic Bladder & Bowel 

a flaccid **or** spastic bowel.  These patients can experience a combination of both reflexic and areflexic bowel symptoms. These patients treatment should follow the arreflexive pattern initially and the specialist spinal nurses should be contacted via the neurosurgical secretaries for further advice. 

For further information or advice on the management of Neurogenic bowel for patients at **Queen Square** , please contact the complex spine neurosurgical unit secretaries on: 

020 3448 3568/3150/3395 

## **Additional Notes** 

**It is important to note that, acutely after cord injury, spinal shock occurs where all bowels are flaccid, generally for up to 2 weeks (but may be longer or much shorter).  After this time, as the cord recovers, the more permanent clinical picture will arise.** 

**Autonomic Dysreflexia (AD),** is a potentially dangerous complication for patients with a spinal cord injury (SCI) at T6 or above. In AD, an individual's blood pressure may rise to dangerous levels and if not treated can lead to stroke and possibly death.  AD usually occurs because of a noxious (irritating) stimulus below the level of the injury.  Severe constipation / impaction and performing bowel management can occasionally cause AD.  Symptoms include headache, tachycardia or bradycardia, facial flush, perspiration, and a stuffy nose (United Spinal Association, 2015). 

## **Faecal impaction** 

_“Symptoms of constipation (either with or without overflow incontinence), in the presence of hard faecal mass obstructing the rectum or colon”   (Hussain 2014)._

text:
University College London Hospitals 

Management of Spinal Neurogenic Bladder & Bowel **Overflow incontinence -** If a patient is constipated or has faecal impaction, 

the liquid element of stool bypasses the hard impacted stool and leaks out. This is often mistaken for ‘diarrhoea’ by nurses, all laxatives are stopped: this cessation makes the situation worse.  It is therefore imperative that it is understood that overflow incontinence is considered before any changes to medication are made. 

**Digital removal faeces-** If you would like more information on undertaking digital removal faeces (DRF) / digital rectal examination (DRE), please refer to RCN Guidance for nurses: 

‘Management of lower bowel dysfunction, including DRE and DRF’ 

_- - https://www.rcn.org.uk/professional development/publications/pub 003226_ 

## **References** 

Ash, D., (2005). Sustaining safe and acceptable bowel care in spinal cord injured patients. _Nursing Standard_ , _20_ (8), pp.55-64 

Coggrave M (2007) Neurogenic Bowel Management in chronic spinal cord injury; Evidence of nursing care. Unpublished PhD Kings College London (Cited in MASCIP Guideline 2012) 

Coggrave, M., McCreath, A. and Sister, R.D., (2007). Bowel management following spinal cord injury _United Kingdom: Buckinghamshire Healthcare NHS Trust_ 

Coggrave, M., Norton, C. and Cody, J.D.,( 2014) Management of faecal incontinence and constipation in adults with central neurological diseases _The Cochrane Library_ [Online] Available at: http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/14651858.CD002115.pub5/pdf {accessed:  30/03/2017} 

Krassioukov, A., Eng, J.J., Claxton, G., Sakakibara, B.M. and Shum, S., (2010). Neurogenic bowel management after spinal cord injury: a systematic review of the evidence. _Spinal Cord_ , _48_ (10), pp.718-733 

Lewis SJ, Heaton KW (1997) Stool Form scale as a useful guide to intestinal transit time. _Scandinavian Journal of Gastroenterology_ 32: 920-4 

Management of lower bowel dysfunction, including DRE and DRF. RCN guidance for nurses (2012)

text:
Classification: Official 

_Resources to support safer bowel care for patients at risk of autonomic dysreflexia_ 

## **Patient Safety Alert** 

## 25 July 2018 

## **Actions** 

## Alert reference number: NHS/PSA/RE/2018/005 

## Resource Alert 

Patients with spinal cord injury or neurological conditions may have neurogenic bowel dysfunction, which often means they depend on routine interventional bowel care, including the digital (manual) removal of faeces (DRF). 

Some of these patients, especially those with spinal cord injury above T6, are particularly susceptible to the potentially life-threatening condition autonomic dysreflexia, which is characterised by a rapid rise in blood pressure, risking cerebral haemorrhage and death. A small number of patients who have had a severe stroke or who have severe forms of Parkinson’s Disease, multiple sclerosis, cerebral palsy, or spina bifida may also be susceptible to autonomic dysreflexia. 

Autonomic dysreflexia can be caused by non-adherence to a patient's usual bowel routine or during or following interventional bowel care. For all of these patients, bowel care is vital for their health and dignity. 

Patients have made NHS Improvement aware of difficulties ensuring their regular bowel care is provided when they come into hospital or mental health units, or access other NHS care such as community care. For example, one patient said: 

“Despite explaining my situation, I had to wait eight days for an enema. I’d had shoulder surgery so couldn’t do it myself. Had the beginnings of autonomic dysreflexia.” 

A search of the National Reporting and Learning System (NRLS) over a four-and-ahalf-year period identified 61 reports of significant delays in providing DRF or an appropriate alternative, including three cases of autonomic dysreflexia. Reports came from acute hospitals, community services and care homes. 

The key issues appeared to be a lack of staff with the training and experience to perform DRF (particularly as opportunities to learn and practice are limited outside of spinal injuries units and community teams who regularly undertake this procedure), or an inability to identify staff with the appropriate training. 

The search also indicated: 

- unclear local policies stating who could perform DRF, including the patient’s carers or healthcare professionals from another provider 

Who: All providers of NHSfunded inpatient and community healthcare* 

When: To begin as soon as possible and be completed by 25 January 2019 

   - Identify an appropriate clinical 

   - **1** 

   - ra leader to co-ordinate implementation of this alert. 

   - Using the resources referred to 

   - **2** 

   - A in this alert, review your local clinical policy and guidance relating to bowel assessment and management. 

   - Review your local education and 

   - **3** 

   - A training provision for interventional bowel management, and develop an action plan to ensure patients have adequate and timely access to staff who are appropriately trained to carry out these procedures, including in the evening and at weekends. 

   - Share your reviewed local guidance, 

   - **4** 

   - A advice on how to identify staff who can provide DRF, and the key messages in this alert with medical, nursing and other relevant clinical staff. 

- lack of knowledge of relevant clinical guidance 

- uncertainty over requirement for and provision of training 

- uncertainty over using alternative methods of bowel management 

- a mistaken belief that this type of care constitutes assault. 

Despite a previous Patient Safety Alert,[1] national clinical guidance,[2,3 ] professionspecific guidance[4,5,6,7] and patient resources,[8] issues persist that cause patients distress and can put them at risk of severe harm or death. Providers have indicated that additional resources to develop and maintain staff skills in this area would be helpful. This alert provides links to a range of resources[9] to support safer bowel care for patients at risk of autonomic dysreflexia, and highlights the publication of NHS England’s Excellence in continence care guidance 2018[10] which addresses how providers can overcome implementation challenges. 

*GP’s would not be expected to lead on policy/ training actions but should be aware of the need to act quickly, and appropriately escalate care for affected patients. 

## **Sharing resources and examples of work** 

If you are aware of any resources or examples of work developed in relation to this alert that you think would be useful to others, please share them with us by emailing patientsafety.enquiries@nhs.net 

Patient Safety See page 2 for references, stakeholder engagement and ———LLLL—=_ improvement.nhs.uk/resources/patient-safety-alerts advice on who this alert should be directed to. 

Contact us: patientsafety.enquiries@nhs.net 

Publication code: IT 09/18 

NHS Improvement (July 2018)

text:
Classification: Official 

Alert reference number: NHS/PSA/RE/2018/005 

## Resource Alert 

## **NRLS search dates and terms** 

The NRLS was searched for incidents occurring on or after 13 May 2013 if reported by 19 May 2016, and this search was updated for incidents occurring on or after 13 May 2013 if reported between 19 May 2016 and 1 December 2017, using the search terms [%stool% or %faeces% or %bowel%] AND [%manual% or %digital%] AND [%remov% or %evac%]. This combined search yielded 61 relevant reports describing difficulties providing appropriate bowel care, including three incidents where autonomic dysreflexia had occurred. 

## **References** 

1. National Patient Safety Agency 2004 Patient briefing and patient information notice ‘bowel care for patients with established spinal cord lesions’ http://webarchive.nationalarchives.gov.uk/20171030124321/http://www. nrls.npsa.nhs.uk/resources/?entryid45=59790&p=16 

2. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence clinical guideline 2014 [CG49] Faecal incontinence in adults: management https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/cg49 

3. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence quality standard 2014 [QS 54] Faecal incontinence in adults https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/qs54 

4. British Association of Spinal Cord Injury Specialists (BASCIS), Multidisciplinary Association of Spinal Cord Injury Professionals (MASCIP), Spinal Injuries Association (SIA) 2014 Statement on Autonomic Dysreflexia https://www. spinal.co.uk/wp-content/uploads/2018/06/Statement-on-Autonomic-Dysreflexia-2017.pdf 

5. Royal College of Nursing 2012 Management of lower bowel dysfunction, including digital rectal examination and digital removal of faeces. https://www.rcn.org.uk/professional-development/publications/pub-003226 

6. Nursing and Midwifery Council 2018. Future nurse: Standards of proficiency for registered nurses https://www. nmc.org.uk/globalassets/sitedocuments/education-standards/future-nurse-proficiencies.pdf 

7. Multidisciplinary Association of Spinal Cord Injured Professionals 2012 Guidelines for management of neurogenic bowel dysfunction in individuals with central neurological conditions http://www.mascip.co.uk/wpcontent/uploads/2015/02/CV653N-Neurogenic-Guidelines-Sept-2012.pdf 

8. Spinal Injury Association 2013 Patient and professional resources https://www.spinal.co.uk/wp-content/ uploads/2017/05/Autonomic-Dysreflexia.pdf 

9. NHS Improvement 2018, Resources to support safer bowel care for patients at risk of autonomic dysreflexia webpage https://improvement.nhs.uk/resources/resources-to-support-safer-bowel-care-for-patients-at-risk-ofautonomic-dysreflexia 

10. NHS England (updated 2018) Excellence in continence care framework https://www.england.nhs.uk/publication/ excellence-in-continence-care/ 

## **Resources** 

- The resources to support the implementation of this Alert are available on the NHS Improvement website https://improvement.nhs.uk/resources/resources-to-support-safer-bowel-care-for-patients-at-risk-of-autonomicdysreflexia 

## **Stakeholder engagement** 

- Royal College of Nursing Continence Forum 

- NHS England Excellence in Continence Care Committee 

- The Spinal Injuries Association 

- National Patient Safety Response Advisory Panel (for a list of members and organisations represented on the panel, see improvement.nhs.uk/resources/patient-safety-alerts/) 

## **Advice for Central Alerting System officers and risk managers** 

This alert is particularly relevant to all healthcare services where patients with existing spinal cord injury may need care for other conditions, and is likely to include community nursing services and mental health inpatient units, as well as acute hospitals for adults and children, and care homes. 

This alert requires central co-ordination of a revision to local policies and training, so should not be widely circulated until these have been developed. 

If you are unsure who to direct this alert to, your local continence nurse specialist will be able to signpost the medical, nursing and allied health professional staff and educational leads who will coordinate local implementation of this alert. 

Patient Safety improvement.nhs.uk/resources/patient-safety-alerts 

Contact us: patientsafety.enquiries@nhs.net

text:
## The National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery Neuroscience Critical Care Unit (NCCU) 

- **LABETALOL HYDROCHLORIDE** for **RAPID CONTROL** of **HYPERTENSION** 

## **INDICATIONS FOR USE** 

Severe hypertension when rapid control of blood pressure is essential. 

## **STABILITY AND COMPATIBILITY** 

_See MEDUSA INJECTABLE MEDICINES GUIDE_ 

## **CONTRAINDICATIONS** 

C ontraindicated in second or third degree heart block (unless pacemaker is in situ), cardiogenic shock and other conditions associated with severe and prolonged hypotension or severe bradycardia. 

## **ACUTE REACTIONS** 

- Bradycardia (excessive bradycardia can be reversed with atropine) 

- `o` Heart block, heart failure 

- `o` Hypotension, postural hypotension 

- `o` Bronchospasm 

- `o` Peripheral coldness, paraesthesia 

- `o` Hypersensitivity reactions 

## **EXTRAVASATION** 

- Ideally administered using an infusion pump, via a central venous access device 

- `o` Labetalol has a low pH and may cause venous irritation and tissue damage in cases of extravasation. 

- If a central venous access device is unavailable, administer via a large peripheral vein monitoring insertion site closely using a recognised phlebitis scoring tool. 

- `o` Re-site cannula at first signs of inflammation 

## **INTRAVENOUS LABETALOL as 1[st] LINE DRUG for RAPID CONTROL of HYPERTENSION** 

## **PRESENTATION and ADMINISTRATION** 

1. 1 ampoule contains labetalol **50mg in 10mL** (5mg/mL) 

2. IV INJECTION **:** Give **NEAT as 5mg/mL** solution 

3. IV INFUSION: Prepare as an **UNDILUTED 5mg/mL** solution preferably via a central venous access device 

_4. Labetalol can be diluted to a 1mg/mL solution for both bolus and infusion if necessary to give smaller amounts – preferred diluent is 5% glucose but 0.9% sodium chloride can also be used_ 

## **BOLUS DOSE** 

- **STEP 1** _**ONLY USE IF ESSENTIAL TO RAPIDLY REDUCE BP IN HYPERTENSIVE EMERGENCY**_ `o` Bolus dose of up to **50mg** can be given by I.V. injection over 1 minute 

- `o` Repeat at 5 - 10 minute intervals until satisfactory response 

- `o` Total dose should not exceed 200mg in 24 hours (maximum 4 doses) 

- `o` Maximum effect usually occurs within 5 minutes and the duration of action is usually about 6 hours 

- **STEP 2 INFUSION** `o` Start infusion at **4mL/h** (20mg/h) `o` Titrate to BP target by adjusting - doubling, maintaining or halving - the infusion as required every 15 to 30 minutes – _allow time to take effect before making adjustments to avoid large swings in BP_ 

- `o` If BP decreases precipitously, halve the infusion rate or cease depending on severity 

- `o` Maximum dose 160mg/h (32mL/h) – _if BP target not reached consider other agent (2[nd] line: Hydralazine)_ 

- **STEP 3 WEANING** `o` Discontinue by weaning over 1 - 2 hours when clinically appropriate 

- `o` Allow each decrease to take effect before making further adjustments to avoid a precipitous increase in BP 

- `o` Oral / NG treatment started, IV therapy weaned and stopped within 2-3 days 

**RAPID CONTROL OF BLOOD PRESSURE IN ACUTE STROKE** ACUTE ISCHAEMIC STROKE (AIS) _POST_ <185/110mmHg IV Thrombolysis _POST_ Thrombectomy +/<185/110mmHg IV Thrombol sis y _NEITHER_ IV Thrombolysis nor <220/120 Thrombectom y NCCU <185mmHg Management HAEMORRHAGIC STROKE <140/90mmHg for at least 7 days Aim to achieve **target BP within 1 hour** of starting treatment – _while ensuring it does not drop >60mmHg within 1h_ RAPID BP LOWERING should be avoided if elevated ICP suspected, GCS <6 or neurosurgical evacuation endin p g 

THE NATIONAL HOSPITAL FOR NEUROLOGY AND NEUROSURGERY JULY 2023

text:
## **MRI for Neurocritical Care Patients** 

Please ensure all of the steps below are followed when a neurocritical care (NCC) patient requires MRI 

## **STEP 1** BOOK PATIENT FOR MRI ON **PINK EMERGENCY LIST** - 2[ND] FLOOR THEATRE RECEPTION 

**STEP 2** CONTACT THEATRE CO-ORDINATOR - 07967 773925 **PLUS** 2[nd] ON CALL ANAESTHETIC SpR - BLEEP 8011 

**STEP 3** _MRI may already be booked by_ BOOK TIME SLOT WITH MRI _Neurosurgery /_ - EXT 83581 / 83074 _Neurology / Stroke_ **STEP 4** INFORM BOTH NCC NURSE-IN-CHARGE & BEDSIDE NURSE THAT PATIENT HAS BEEN BOOKED FOR MRI 

_Bedside nurse prepares patient for MRI with line extensions, etc_ 

NCC NURSE MUST ESCORT **ALL** PATIENTS TO & FROM MRI 

## **Awake Patients on infusions** 

- ODP will attend MRI to connect monitoring and help with pumps, but it is not their role to monitor and be in sole charge of the patient - NCC nurse in charge + ITU consultant will decide whether anaesthetic escort (2[nd] on SpR) is required in addition to NCC nurse for ‘out of hours’ MRI for patient on inotropes 

Dr Sally Wilson 

THE NATIONAL HOSPITAL FOR NEUROLOGY AND NEUROSURGERY APRIL 2022

text:
## The National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery Neuroscience Critical Care Unit (NCCU) - **DOUBLE PUMPING VASOACTIVE DRUGS** in **NEUROCRITICAL CARE** _**Adrenaline noradrenaline** and_ _**dobutamine** , ,_ _**dopamine** have very short biological half lives (1-2 minutes) due to reuptake in tissues -_ _**these drugs must be double pumped at all times.** There must be uninterrupted administration of these drugs, thus one infusion is commenced when the other is almost empty, this is termed ‘double pumping’ or ‘piggybacking’ – any interruption in flow will result in haemodynamic instability and can cause cardiac arrest. Drugs with a longer half-life do not require double pumping._ ## **EXCLUSIONS** ## **DOUBLE PUMPING GUIDELINE** **==> picture [2262 x 1166] intentionally omitted <==** **----- Start of picture text -----**

text:
The National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery Neuroscience Critical Care Unit (NCCU) 

Selectively inhibits cAMP-specific phosphodiesterase III isoenzyme in cardiac and vascular muscle. Vasodilation occurs due to increase in cAMP in vascular smooth muscle which facilitates calcium uptake, reducing amount of calcium available for contractility.  This vasodilation can directly contribute to reducing vasospasm. 

## **PHARMACOKINETICS** 

Milrinone is a selective phosphodiesterase-3 inhibitor associated with circulatory inotropic support and selective cerebral–vasodilatory effects and should be considered in cerebral vasospasm with delayed cerebral ischaemia (DCI) which is refractory to standard therapy. 

- Onset of action 5 -15min 

- • Metabolised by liver 

- • Half-life 2.5h 

- • Excreted in urine 

## **INDICATIONS FOR USE** 

- Suspected DCI 

- Refractory vasospasm confirmed with CTA, 2 

- TCD or low brain oxygen tension (PbtO ) 

- □ Vasospasm confirmed at DSA 

- Diffuse vasospasm confirmed on CTA without an interventional radiology (IR) target 

- □ Treatment of vasospasm when IR not available 

- □ Vasospasm with focal neurology not responsive to hypertension / angioplasty 

- **REQUIREMENTS PRIOR TO COMMENCING** Tn **MILRINONE** ~~—_—~~ □ Cardiac output monitoring / Central line 

- □ 2 Brain tissue oxygen (PbtO ) probe in sedated some 

- patient / Clinical signs in awake patient – awake patients may be electively sedated and intubated to facilitate PbtO2 bolt + milrinone – _particularly if evidence of poor cardiac function and on maximum pressors_ 

- □ Noradrenaline / vasopressin infusion 

- □ Euvolaemia 

- Hb > 90g/dl 

- □ PaO2 > 13kPa 

References 1.Yasser B. Abulhasan et al Milrinone for refractory cerebral vasospasm with delayed cerebral ischemia, J Neurosurg March 27, 2020 2.Marcelo Lannes et al Milrinone and Homeostasis to Treat Cerebral Vasospasm Associated with SAH: Neurocrit Care (2012) 16:354–362

text:
THE NATIONAL HOSPITAL FOR NEUROLOGY AND NEUROSURGERY **BLOWN PUPIL PATHWAY** 

**This pathway is only to be applied to patients at The National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery** 

A “ **blown pupil** ” is a medical emergency indicating trans-tentorial herniation and brainstem compression. This can be caused by a variety of surgical . reversible pathologies such as haematoma, hydrocephalus, large volume infarct or oedema surrounding tumour or infection Rapid access to surgical evacuation of extra-axial haematomas significantly improves outcomes and is the most important factor in improving prognosis.  Progression to . definitive surgical intervention must occur within ‘the golden hour’ to prevent secondary brain injury and irreversible brainstem ischaemia MINIMUM ACTIVATION CRITERIA 

## **BLOWN PUPIL** 

## To meet minimum activation criteria the patient must have: 

1. Impaired consciousness (GCS <15) 

2. At least one pupil that is both FIXED (completely unreactive to light) and DILATED (>5mm) 

3. A reasonable clinical suspicion that pupillary dilatation is secondary to intracranial mass effect 

ACTIVATION 

- The pathway can be activated by any member of staff trained in making above clinical assessment of pupils and medical context 

- For patients being transferred from another hospital this will be activated by the on call Neurosurgery Registrar, including dissemination of estimated time of arrival (ETA) and clinical details 

- The pathway is activated via the **SWITCHBOARD OPERATOR** through the emergency number **2222** 

- On speaking to the operator the activator should request a **‘Blown Pupil’ call is activated** _- this will send a fast bleep/phone call to the following:_ 

TEAM MEMBERS 

- On call Neurosurgery Registrar 

- On call Neurosurgery SHO 

- First On call Anaesthetic Registrar 

- On call Radiographer 

- Theatre Coordinator _(in hours)_ or on call Theatre Team _(out of hours)_ 

- Neurocritical Care Outreach 

**Confirmation of blown pupil and reduced level of consciousness BLOWN PUPIL code activated via 2222** 

**IN HOURS** 

## **OUT OF HOURS** 

**Theatre Team identify an appropriate theatre and prepare A B C D E management of patient** 

**Theatre Team and Radiographer make their way into hospital A B C D E management of patient Urgent transfer to CT (as soon as possible) Decision for surgical or medical management by neurosurgery SpR** 

- A. Intubate _(if not already)_ for transfer B. Ventilate: Pa02 11-13kPa / PaCO2 4.5-5Kpa C. Gain IV access, send bloods (FBC, U&Es, clotting, G&S, BM). Arterial line can be placed at the bedside only if it does not cause a delay in transfer – **DO NOT WAIT** for this prior to transfer 

**Urgent transfer to CT** 

## **Decision for surgical or medical management by neurosurgery SpR** 

- D. GCS / pupil assessment, document and start 2.7% hypertonic saline or 15% mannitol for osmotherapy _(see below)_ 

- E. Catheters and central lines should **NOT Transfer from CT to** 

- routinely be placed until the patient reaches the operating theatre as the **Theatre for surgery or to** priority is rapid transfer for neurosurgery **NCC for bolt EVD** _**(as soon as possible)**_ 

**Transfer from CT to Theatre for surgery or to NCC for bolt EVD** _**(within 1 hour of activation)**_ 

**To Theatre for surgery or to Neurocritical Care (NCC) for medical management** 

- Neurocritical Care Nurse-in-Charge 

- On call Radiology Registrar 

- Porter 

- Neurosurgery Consultant _(Daytime hours only). Neurosurgery registrar to liaise with consultant out of hours_ 

- Neurocritical Care Consultant _(Daytime hours only)_ . _Anaesthetic registrar to liaise with consultant out of hours_ 

- • Anaesthetic Floor Consultant / On call consultant _(Daytime hours_ . 

- _only) Anaesthetic registrar to liaise with consultant out of hours_ 

## **NEUROPROTECTIVE MEASURES** 

_Standard neuroprotective measures should be undertaken_ Pa02 11-13kPa Levetiracetam 1g IV if PaCO2 4.5 – 5kPa suspicion of seizure activity No neck ties obstructing MAP >80mmHg; _aiming for_ venous outflow _CPP >65mmHg_ 2 >95% Head up to 30 degrees SpO 

## **HYPEROSMOLAR TREATMENT** 

Either 2.7% hypertonic saline or 15% mannitol given as directed by neurosurgeon or anaesthetist via **large bore cannula** (14G or 16G) as stat dose over **30min** followed by reassessment of pupils before further dosing 

► **1[st] line: 2.7% Hypertonic Saline** (3-5ml/kg) - **Initial stat dose of 210mL over 30min** - In a typical 70kg patient 210mL = 3ml/kg. _**DO NOT** give to patients with known hypernatraemia (Serum Sodium >155mmol/L)_ ► **15% Mannitol (** 0.5-1g/kg) **- Initial stat dose of 230mL over 30min** - In a typical 70kg patient 230mL = 0.5g/kg 

Patrick Grover, Iby Adedugbe, Will Muirhead 

THE NATIONAL HOSPITAL FOR NEUROLOGY AND NEUROSURGERY JULY 2024
